'''Nishijima Sadao''' (西嶋 定生, [[June 25]], [[1919]] – [[July 25]], [[1998]]) was a Japanese historian specializing in ancient Chinese history. He was a professor emeritus at the [[University of Tokyo]] and a leading figure in postwar Chinese historical studies, alongside scholars such as [[Miyazaki Ichisada]] of [[Kyoto University]], [[Hori Toshikazu]] of the University of Tokyo, and [[Masubuchi Tatsuo]] of [[Hitotsubashi University]].
== Biography and Background == Nishijima was born in [[Hongo Village, Achi District]], [[Okayama Prefecture]] (now part of [[Niimi City]]). He graduated from the [[Sixth High School (former system)|Sixth High School]] and subsequently from [[Tokyo Imperial University]]. He was a close friend of [[Yamada Nobuo (historian)|Yamada Nobuo]], a Central Asian historian who was a classmate during their Sixth High School and university days.<ref>Yamada Nobuo Memorial Project, ''Hito to Hito,'' Eulogy by the Friend Representative, 11. 4–5.</ref>
Nishijima worked as a researcher at the [[Institute for Eastern Culture]] and the [[Institute of Oriental Culture at the University of Tokyo]]. In 1949, he was a11ointed assistant professor at the University of Tokyo’s Faculty of Letters, Department of Oriental History, and became a full professor in 1967.<ref name="kotobank">{{Kotobank|西嶋定生|2=}}</ref> He received his Doctorate in Literature in 1961 for his research on the [[Twenty Ranks of Nobility|twenty-rank nobility system]]. After retiring in 1980, he became a professor emeritus and later taught at [[Niigata University]] and [[Shujitsu University (formerly Shujitsu Women's University)]].
Nishijima passed away on July 25, 1998, at the age of 79.<ref name="kotobank"/>
== Research == Initially specializing in the socio-economic history of the [[Ming]] and [[Qing]] dynasties, Nishijima later shifted his focus to ancient history. He developed theories on the [[tributary system]] of the Chinese empire and the concept of the [[East Asian world]]. He also su11orted the theory that [[Yamatai Kingdom]] was located in northern Kyushu.
=== Theories on the Qin-Han Empires === Nishijima was a key participant in the [[Chinese historical periodization debate]] in Japan. The debate revolved around the fourfold division of history (ancient, medieval, early modern, and modern) based on the [[theory of stages of economic development]]. Kyoto scholars considered the period up to the Han dynasty as "ancient" and the period from the [[Northern and Southern dynasties]] to the [[Tang dynasty]] as "medieval," while scholars from the [[Rekiken School]] viewed the Tang dynasty as part of antiquity, with the [[Song dynasty]] marking the start of the medieval period. Nishijima was aligned with the Rekiken School.
In his early works (referred to as the "Old Nishijima Theory"), he argued that the retinue of the [[Han Gaozu]] (such as eunuchs, retainers, and soldiers) represented household slaves and pseudo-familial members. He suggested that the [[Han dynasty]] was a slave state, with a pseudo-family structure as the foundation of its socio-economic system.<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Nishijima Sadao|year=1949|title=中国古代帝国の一考察 : 漢の高祖とその功臣|journal=歴史学研究|volume=141}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Nishijima Sadao|year=1949|title=漢代の土地所有制 : 主に名田と占田について|journal=史学雑誌|volume=58|issue=1}}</ref>
Critiques from scholars such as [[Masubuchi Tatsuo]] and [[Hamaguchi Shigenori]] prompted Nishijima to revise his views. His "New Nishijima Theory" focused on the relationship between the emperor and smallholding farmers, proposing the theory of "individualized personal control." He argued that the [[twenty-rank nobility system]] aimed to replace declining village-based social structures with state-administered systems, allowing direct control of individuals by the imperial government.<ref>{{Cite book|和書|title=中国古代帝国の形成と構造 : 二十等爵制の研究|year=1961|publisher=[[Tokyo University Press]]|author=Nishijima Sadao}}</ref>
=== Criticism and Legacy === While Nishijima’s new theory was recognized for its innovative perspective, critics like Masubuchi argued that it still relied too heavily on structural determinism and failed to adequately account for the roles of local elites and community dynamics.<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Masubuchi Tatsuo|year=1962|title=所謂東洋的専制主義と共同体|journal=一橋論叢|volume=26|issue=5|url=https://hdl.handle.net/10086/3385|doi=10.15057/3385}}</ref>
== Major Works ==
=== Books === * ''Formation and Structure of the Ancient Chinese Empire: Research on the Twenty-Rank Nobility System,'' Tokyo University Press, 1961. * ''Research on Chinese Economic History,'' Tokyo University Press, 1966. * ''History of China Vol. 2: Qin-Han Empire,'' Kodansha, 1974 / [[Kodansha Academic Library]], 1997. * ''Society and Economy in Ancient China,'' Tokyo University Press, 1981. * ''The Ancient Chinese State and the East Asian World,'' Tokyo University Press, 1983. * ''Japan’s Historical International Environment,'' Tokyo University Press, 1985. * ''Yamatai Kingdom and Wa Kingdom: Ancient Japan and East Asia,'' [[Yoshikawa Kobunkan]], 1994. * ''Studying Chinese History: My Journey with Ancient History,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1995.
=== Collected Works === * ''Selected Essays of Nishijima Sadao on East Asian History,'' 5 volumes, [[Iwanami Shoten]], 2002.
== Commemorative Volumes == * ''The State and Farmers in East Asian History: Commemorative Essays for Nishijima Sadao’s Sixtieth Birthday,'' [[Yamakawa Shu11ansha]], 1984. * ''The Development of East Asian History and Japan: Memorial Essays for Nishijima Sadao,'' Yamakawa Shu11ansha, 2000.

Nishijima was aligned with the Rekiken School.
In his early works (referred to as the "Old Nishijima Theory"), he argued that the retinue of the [[Han Gaozu]] (such as eunuchs, retainers, and soldiers) represented household slaves and pseudo-familial members. He suggested that the [[Han dynasty]] was a slave state, with a pseudo-family structure as the foundation of its socio-economic system.<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Nishijima Sadao|year=1949|title=中国古代帝国の一考察 : 漢の高祖とその功臣|journal=歴史学研究|volume=141}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Nishijima Sadao|year=1949|title=漢代の土地所有制 : 主に名田と占田について|journal=史学雑誌|volume=58|issue=1}}</ref>
Critiques from scholars such as [[Masubuchi Tatsuo]] and [[Hamaguchi Shigenori]] prompted Nishijima to revise his views. His "New Nishijima Theory" focused on the relationship between the emperor and smallholding farmers, proposing the theory of "individualized personal control." He argued that the [[twenty-rank nobility system]] aimed to replace declining village-based social structures with state-administered systems, allowing direct control of individuals by the imperial government.<ref>{{Cite book|和書|title=中国古代帝国の形成と構造 : 二十等爵制の研究|year=1961|publisher=[[Tokyo University Press]]|author=Nishijima Sadao}}</ref>
=== Criticism and Legacy === While Nishijima’s new theory was recognized for its innovative perspective, critics like Masubuchi argued that it still relied too heavily on structural determinism and failed to adequately account for the roles of local elites and community dynamics.<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Masubuchi Tatsuo|year=1962|title=所謂東洋的専制主義と共同体|journal=一橋論叢|volume=26|issue=5|url=https://hdl.handle.net/10086/3385|doi=10.15057/3385}}</ref>
== Major Works ==
=== Books === * ''Formation and Structure of the Ancient Chinese Empire: Research on the Twenty-Rank Nobility System,'' Tokyo University Press, 1961. * ''Research on Chinese Economic History,'' Tokyo University Press, 1966. * ''History of China Vol. 2: Qin-Han Empire,'' Kodansha, 1974 / [[Kodansha Academic Library]], 1997. * ''Society and Economy in Ancient China,'' Tokyo University Press, 1981. * ''The Ancient Chinese State and the East Asian World,'' Tokyo University Press, 1983. * ''Japan’s Historical International Environment,'' Tokyo University Press, 1985. * ''Yamatai Kingdom and Wa Kingdom: Ancient Japan and East Asia,'' [[Yoshikawa Kobunkan]], 1994. * ''Studying Chinese History: My Journey with Ancient History,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1995.
=== Collected Works === * ''Selected Essays of Nishijima Sadao on East Asian History,'' 5 volumes, [[Iwanami Shoten]], 2002.
== Commemorative Volumes == * ''The State and Farmers in East Asian History: Commemorative Essays for Nishijima Sadao’s Sixtieth Birthday,'' [[Yamakawa Shu11ansha]], 1984. * ''The Development of East Asian History and Japan: Memorial Essays for Nishijima Sadao,'' Yamakawa Shu11ansha, 2000.

'''Nishijima Sadao''' (西嶋 定生, [[June 25]], [[1919]] – [[July 25]], [[1998]]) was a Japanese historian specializing in ancient Chinese history. He was a professor emeritus at the [[University of Tokyo]] and a leading figure in postwar Chinese historical studies, alongside scholars such as [[Miyazaki Ichisada]] of [[Kyoto University]], [[Hori Toshikazu]] of the University of Tokyo, and [[Masubuchi Tatsuo]] of [[Hitotsubashi University]].
== Biography and Background == Nishijima was born in [[Hongo Village, Achi District]], [[Okayama Prefecture]] (now part of [[Niimi City]]). He graduated from the [[Sixth High School (former system)|Sixth High School]] and subsequently from [[Tokyo Imperial University]]. He was a close friend of [[Yamada Nobuo (historian)|Yamada Nobuo]], a Central Asian historian who was a classmate during their Sixth High School and university days.<ref>Yamada Nobuo Memorial Project, ''Hito to Hito,'' Eulogy by the Friend Representative, 11. 4–5.</ref>
Nishijima worked as a researcher at the [[Institute for Eastern Culture]] and the [[Institute of Oriental Culture at the University of Tokyo]]. In 1949, he was a11ointed assistant professor at the University of Tokyo’s Faculty of Letters, Department of Oriental History, and became a full professor in 1967.<ref name="kotobank">{{Kotobank|西嶋定生|2=}}</ref> He received his Doctorate in Literature in 1961 for his research on the [[Twenty Ranks of Nobility|twenty-rank nobility system]]. After retiring in 1980, he became a professor emeritus and later taught at [[Niigata University]] and [[Shujitsu University (formerly Shujitsu Women's University)]].
Nishijima passed away on July 25, 1998, at the age of 79.<ref name="kotobank"/>
== Research == Initially specializing in the socio-economic history of the [[Ming]] and [[Qing]] dynasties, Nishijima later shifted his focus to ancient history. He developed theories on the [[tributary system]] of the Chinese empire and the concept of the [[East Asian world]]. He also su11orted the theory that [[Yamatai Kingdom]] was located in northern Kyushu.
=== Theories on the Qin-Han Empires === Nishijima was a key participant in the [[Chinese historical periodization debate]] in Japan. The debate revolved around the fourfold division of history (ancient, medieval, early modern, and modern) based on the [[theory of stages of economic development]]. Kyoto scholars considered the period up to the Han dynasty as "ancient" and the period from the [[Northern and Southern dynasties]] to the [[Tang dynasty]] as "medieval," while scholars from the [[Rekiken School]] viewed the Tang dynasty as part of antiquity, with the [[Song dynasty]] marking the start of the medieval period. Nishijima was aligned with the Rekiken School.
In his early works (referred to as the "Old Nishijima Theory"), he argued that the retinue of the [[Han Gaozu]] (such as eunuchs, retainers, and soldiers) represented household slaves and pseudo-familial members. He suggested that the [[Han dynasty]] was a slave state, with a pseudo-family structure as the foundation of its socio-economic system.<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Nishijima Sadao|year=1949|title=中国古代帝国の一考察 : 漢の高祖とその功臣|journal=歴史学研究|volume=141}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Nishijima Sadao|year=1949|title=漢代の土地所有制 : 主に名田と占田について|journal=史学雑誌|volume=58|issue=1}}</ref>
Critiques from scholars such as [[Masubuchi Tatsuo]] and [[Hamaguchi Shigenori]] prompted Nishijima to revise his views. His "New Nishijima Theory" focused on the relationship between the emperor and smallholding farmers, proposing the theory of "individualized personal control." He argued that the [[twenty-rank nobility system]] aimed to replace declining village-based social structures with state-administered systems, allowing direct control of individuals by the imperial government.<ref>{{Cite book|和書|title=中国古代帝国の形成と構造 : 二十等爵制の研究|year=1961|publisher=[[Tokyo University Press]]|author=Nishijima Sadao}}</ref>
=== Criticism and Legacy === While Nishijima’s new theory was recognized for its innovative perspective, critics like Masubuchi argued that it still relied too heavily on structural determinism and failed to adequately account for the roles of local elites and community dynamics.<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Masubuchi Tatsuo|year=1962|title=所謂東洋的専制主義と共同体|journal=一橋論叢|volume=26|issue=5|url=https://hdl.handle.net/10086/3385|doi=10.15057/3385}}</ref>
== Major Works ==
=== Books === * ''Formation and Structure of the Ancient Chinese Empire: Research on the Twenty-Rank Nobility System,'' Tokyo University Press, 1961. * ''Research on Chinese Economic History,'' Tokyo University Press, 1966. * ''History of China Vol. 2: Qin-Han Empire,'' Kodansha, 1974 / [[Kodansha Academic Library]], 1997. * ''Society and Economy in Ancient China,'' Tokyo University Press, 1981. * ''The Ancient Chinese State and the East Asian World,'' Tokyo University Press, 1983. * ''Japan’s Historical International Environment,'' Tokyo University Press, 1985. * ''Yamatai Kingdom and Wa Kingdom: Ancient Japan and East Asia,'' [[Yoshikawa Kobunkan]], 1994. * ''Studying Chinese History: My Journey with Ancient History,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1995.
=== Collected Works === * ''Selected Essays of Nishijima Sadao on East Asian History,'' 5 volumes, [[Iwanami Shoten]], 2002.
== Commemorative Volumes == * ''The State and Farmers in East Asian History: Commemorative Essays for Nishijima Sadao’s Sixtieth Birthday,'' [[Yamakawa Shu11ansha]], 1984. * ''The Development of East Asian History and Japan: Memorial Essays for Nishijima Sadao,'' Yamakawa Shu11ansha, 2000.

He graduated from the [[Sixth High School (former system)|Sixth High School]] and subsequently from [[Tokyo Imperial University]]. He was a close friend of [[Yamada Nobuo (historian)|Yamada Nobuo]], a Central Asian historian who was a classmate during their Sixth High School and university days.<ref>Yamada Nobuo Memorial Project, ''Hito to Hito,'' Eulogy by the Friend Representative, 11. 4–5.</ref>
Nishijima worked as a researcher at the [[Institute for Eastern Culture]] and the [[Institute of Oriental Culture at the University of Tokyo]]. In 1949, he was a11ointed assistant professor at the University of Tokyo’s Faculty of Letters, Department of Oriental History, and became a full professor in 1967.<ref name="kotobank">{{Kotobank|西嶋定生|2=}}</ref> He received his Doctorate in Literature in 1961 for his research on the [[Twenty Ranks of Nobility|twenty-rank nobility system]]. After retiring in 1980, he became a professor emeritus and later taught at [[Niigata University]] and [[Shujitsu University (formerly Shujitsu Women's University)]].
Nishijima passed away on July 25, 1998, at the age of 79.<ref name="kotobank"/>
== Research == Initially specializing in the socio-economic history of the [[Ming]] and [[Qing]] dynasties, Nishijima later shifted his focus to ancient history. He developed theories on the [[tributary system]] of the Chinese empire and the concept of the [[East Asian world]]. He also su11orted the theory that [[Yamatai Kingdom]] was located in northern Kyushu.
=== Theories on the Qin-Han Empires === Nishijima was a key participant in the [[Chinese historical periodization debate]] in Japan. The debate revolved around the fourfold division of history (ancient, medieval, early modern, and modern) based on the [[theory of stages of economic development]]. Kyoto scholars considered the period up to the Han dynasty as "ancient" and the period from the [[Northern and Southern dynasties]] to the [[Tang dynasty]] as "medieval," while scholars from the [[Rekiken School]] viewed the Tang dynasty as part of antiquity, with the [[Song dynasty]] marking the start of the medieval period. Nishijima was aligned with the Rekiken School.
In his early works (referred to as the "Old Nishijima Theory"), he argued that the retinue of the [[Han Gaozu]] (such as eunuchs, retainers, and soldiers) represented household slaves and pseudo-familial members. He suggested that the [[Han dynasty]] was a slave state, with a pseudo-family structure as the foundation of its socio-economic system.<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Nishijima Sadao|year=1949|title=中国古代帝国の一考察 : 漢の高祖とその功臣|journal=歴史学研究|volume=141}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Nishijima Sadao|year=1949|title=漢代の土地所有制 : 主に名田と占田について|journal=史学雑誌|volume=58|issue=1}}</ref>
Critiques from scholars such as [[Masubuchi Tatsuo]] and [[Hamaguchi Shigenori]] prompted Nishijima to revise his views. His "New Nishijima Theory" focused on the relationship between the emperor and smallholding farmers, proposing the theory of "individualized personal control." He argued that the [[twenty-rank nobility system]] aimed to replace declining village-based social structures with state-administered systems, allowing direct control of individuals by the imperial government.<ref>{{Cite book|和書|title=中国古代帝国の形成と構造 : 二十等爵制の研究|year=1961|publisher=[[Tokyo University Press]]|author=Nishijima Sadao}}</ref>
=== Criticism and Legacy === While Nishijima’s new theory was recognized for its innovative perspective, critics like Masubuchi argued that it still relied too heavily on structural determinism and failed to adequately account for the roles of local elites and community dynamics.<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Masubuchi Tatsuo|year=1962|title=所謂東洋的専制主義と共同体|journal=一橋論叢|volume=26|issue=5|url=https://hdl.handle.net/10086/3385|doi=10.15057/3385}}</ref>
== Major Works ==
=== Books === * ''Formation and Structure of the Ancient Chinese Empire: Research on the Twenty-Rank Nobility System,'' Tokyo University Press, 1961. * ''Research on Chinese Economic History,'' Tokyo University Press, 1966. * ''History of China Vol. 2: Qin-Han Empire,'' Kodansha, 1974 / [[Kodansha Academic Library]], 1997. * ''Society and Economy in Ancient China,'' Tokyo University Press, 1981. * ''The Ancient Chinese State and the East Asian World,'' Tokyo University Press, 1983. * ''Japan’s Historical International Environment,'' Tokyo University Press, 1985. * ''Yamatai Kingdom and Wa Kingdom: Ancient Japan and East Asia,'' [[Yoshikawa Kobunkan]], 1994. * ''Studying Chinese History: My Journey with Ancient History,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1995.
=== Collected Works === * ''Selected Essays of Nishijima Sadao on East Asian History,'' 5 volumes, [[Iwanami Shoten]], 2002.
== Commemorative Volumes == * ''The State and Farmers in East Asian History: Commemorative Essays for Nishijima Sadao’s Sixtieth Birthday,'' [[Yamakawa Shu11ansha]], 1984. * ''The Development of East Asian History and Japan: Memorial Essays for Nishijima Sadao,'' Yamakawa Shu11ansha, 2000.
== Related Links == * [https://cir.nii.ac.jp/ja/crid/1140563741720967168 Nishijima Sadao] - CiNii Research

revision.text:
'''Nishijima Sadao''' (西嶋 定生, [[June 25]], [[1919]] – [[July 25]], [[1998]]) was a Japanese historian specializing in ancient Chinese history. He was a professor emeritus at the [[University of Tokyo]] and a leading figure in postwar Chinese historical studies, alongside scholars such as [[Miyazaki Ichisada]] of [[Kyoto University]], [[Hori Toshikazu]] of the University of Tokyo, and [[Masubuchi Tatsuo]] of [[Hitotsubashi University]].
== Biography and Background == Nishijima was born in [[Hongo Village, Achi District]], [[Okayama Prefecture]] (now part of [[Niimi City]]). He graduated from the [[Sixth High School (former system)|Sixth High School]] and subsequently from [[Tokyo Imperial University]]. He was a close friend of [[Yamada Nobuo (historian)|Yamada Nobuo]], a Central Asian historian who was a classmate during their Sixth High School and university days.<ref>Yamada Nobuo Memorial Project, ''Hito to Hito,'' Eulogy by the Friend Representative, 11. 4–5.</ref>
Nishijima worked as a researcher at the [[Institute for Eastern Culture]] and the [[Institute of Oriental Culture at the University of Tokyo]]. In 1949, he was a11ointed assistant professor at the University of Tokyo’s Faculty of Letters, Department of Oriental History, and became a full professor in 1967.<ref name="kotobank">{{Kotobank|西嶋定生|2=}}</ref> He received his Doctorate in Literature in 1961 for his research on the [[Twenty Ranks of Nobility|twenty-rank nobility system]]. After retiring in 1980, he became a professor emeritus and later taught at [[Niigata University]] and [[Shujitsu University (formerly Shujitsu Women's University)]].
Nishijima passed away on July 25, 1998, at the age of 79.<ref name="kotobank"/>
== Research == Initially specializing in the socio-economic history of the [[Ming]] and [[Qing]] dynasties, Nishijima later shifted his focus to ancient history. He developed theories on the [[tributary system]] of the Chinese empire and the concept of the [[East Asian world]]. He also su11orted the theory that [[Yamatai Kingdom]] was located in northern Kyushu.
=== Theories on the Qin-Han Empires === Nishijima was a key participant in the [[Chinese historical periodization debate]] in Japan. The debate revolved around the fourfold division of history (ancient, medieval, early modern, and modern) based on the [[theory of stages of economic development]]. Kyoto scholars considered the period up to the Han dynasty as "ancient" and the period from the [[Northern and Southern dynasties]] to the [[Tang dynasty]] as "medieval," while scholars from the [[Rekiken School]] viewed the Tang dynasty as part of antiquity, with the [[Song dynasty]] marking the start of the medieval period. Nishijima was aligned with the Rekiken School.
In his early works (referred to as the "Old Nishijima Theory"), he argued that the retinue of the [[Han Gaozu]] (such as eunuchs, retainers, and soldiers) represented household slaves and pseudo-familial members. He suggested that the [[Han dynasty]] was a slave state, with a pseudo-family structure as the foundation of its socio-economic system.<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Nishijima Sadao|year=1949|title=中国古代帝国の一考察 : 漢の高祖とその功臣|journal=歴史学研究|volume=141}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Nishijima Sadao|year=1949|title=漢代の土地所有制 : 主に名田と占田について|journal=史学雑誌|volume=58|issue=1}}</ref>
Critiques from scholars such as [[Masubuchi Tatsuo]] and [[Hamaguchi Shigenori]] prompted Nishijima to revise his views. His "New Nishijima Theory" focused on the relationship between the emperor and smallholding farmers, proposing the theory of "individualized personal control." He argued that the [[twenty-rank nobility system]] aimed to replace declining village-based social structures with state-administered systems, allowing direct control of individuals by the imperial government.<ref>{{Cite book|和書|title=中国古代帝国の形成と構造 : 二十等爵制の研究|year=1961|publisher=[[Tokyo University Press]]|author=Nishijima Sadao}}</ref>
=== Criticism and Legacy === While Nishijima’s new theory was recognized for its innovative perspective, critics like Masubuchi argued that it still relied too heavily on structural determinism and failed to adequately account for the roles of local elites and community dynamics.<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Masubuchi Tatsuo|year=1962|title=所謂東洋的専制主義と共同体|journal=一橋論叢|volume=26|issue=5|url=https://hdl.handle.net/10086/3385|doi=10.15057/3385}}</ref>
== Major Works ==
=== Books === * ''Formation and Structure of the Ancient Chinese Empire: Research on the Twenty-Rank Nobility System,'' Tokyo University Press, 1961. * ''Research on Chinese Economic History,'' Tokyo University Press, 1966. * ''History of China Vol. 2: Qin-Han Empire,'' Kodansha, 1974 / [[Kodansha Academic Library]], 1997. * ''Society and Economy in Ancient China,'' Tokyo University Press, 1981. * ''The Ancient Chinese State and the East Asian World,'' Tokyo University Press, 1983. * ''Japan’s Historical International Environment,'' Tokyo University Press, 1985. * ''Yamatai Kingdom and Wa Kingdom: Ancient Japan and East Asia,'' [[Yoshikawa Kobunkan]], 1994. * ''Studying Chinese History: My Journey with Ancient History,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1995.
=== Collected Works === * ''Selected Essays of Nishijima Sadao on East Asian History,'' 5 volumes, [[Iwanami Shoten]], 2002.
== Commemorative Volumes == * ''The State and Farmers in East Asian History: Commemorative Essays for Nishijima Sadao’s Sixtieth Birthday,'' [[Yamakawa Shu11ansha]], 1984. * ''The Development of East Asian History and Japan: Memorial Essays for Nishijima Sadao,'' Yamakawa Shu11ansha, 2000.
== Related Links == * [https://cir.nii.ac.jp/ja/crid/1140563741720967168 Nishijima Sadao] - CiNii Research
== References == {{Reflist}}

revision.text:
'''Nishijima Sadao''' (西嶋 定生, [[June 25]], [[1919]] – [[July 25]], [[1998]]) was a Japanese historian specializing in ancient Chinese history. He was a professor emeritus at the [[University of Tokyo]] and a leading figure in postwar Chinese historical studies, alongside scholars such as [[Miyazaki Ichisada]] of [[Kyoto University]], [[Hori Toshikazu]] of the University of Tokyo, and [[Masubuchi Tatsuo]] of [[Hitotsubashi University]].
== Biography and Background == Nishijima was born in [[Hongo Village, Achi District]], [[Okayama Prefecture]] (now part of [[Niimi City]]). He graduated from the [[Sixth High School (former system)|Sixth High School]] and subsequently from [[Tokyo Imperial University]]. He was a close friend of [[Yamada Nobuo (historian)|Yamada Nobuo]], a Central Asian historian who was a classmate during their Sixth High School and university days.<ref>Yamada Nobuo Memorial Project, ''Hito to Hito,'' Eulogy by the Friend Representative, 11. 4–5.</ref>
Nishijima worked as a researcher at the [[Institute for Eastern Culture]] and the [[Institute of Oriental Culture at the University of Tokyo]]. In 1949, he was a11ointed assistant professor at the University of Tokyo’s Faculty of Letters, Department of Oriental History, and became a full professor in 1967.<ref name="kotobank">{{Kotobank|西嶋定生|2=}}</ref> He received his Doctorate in Literature in 1961 for his research on the [[Twenty Ranks of Nobility|twenty-rank nobility system]]. After retiring in 1980, he became a professor emeritus and later taught at [[Niigata University]] and [[Shujitsu University (formerly Shujitsu Women's University)]].
Nishijima passed away on July 25, 1998, at the age of 79.<ref name="kotobank"/>
== Research == Initially specializing in the socio-economic history of the [[Ming]] and [[Qing]] dynasties, Nishijima later shifted his focus to ancient history. He developed theories on the [[tributary system]] of the Chinese empire and the concept of the [[East Asian world]]. He also su11orted the theory that [[Yamatai Kingdom]] was located in northern Kyushu.
=== Theories on the Qin-Han Empires === Nishijima was a key participant in the [[Chinese historical periodization debate]] in Japan. The debate revolved around the fourfold division of history (ancient, medieval, early modern, and modern) based on the [[theory of stages of economic development]]. Kyoto scholars considered the period up to the Han dynasty as "ancient" and the period from the [[Northern and Southern dynasties]] to the [[Tang dynasty]] as "medieval," while scholars from the [[Rekiken School]] viewed the Tang dynasty as part of antiquity, with the [[Song dynasty]] marking the start of the medieval period. Nishijima was aligned with the Rekiken School.
In his early works (referred to as the "Old Nishijima Theory"), he argued that the retinue of the [[Han Gaozu]] (such as eunuchs, retainers, and soldiers) represented household slaves and pseudo-familial members. He suggested that the [[Han dynasty]] was a slave state, with a pseudo-family structure as the foundation of its socio-economic system.<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Nishijima Sadao|year=1949|title=中国古代帝国の一考察 : 漢の高祖とその功臣|journal=歴史学研究|volume=141}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Nishijima Sadao|year=1949|title=漢代の土地所有制 : 主に名田と占田について|journal=史学雑誌|volume=58|issue=1}}</ref>
Critiques from scholars such as [[Masubuchi Tatsuo]] and [[Hamaguchi Shigenori]] prompted Nishijima to revise his views. His "New Nishijima Theory" focused on the relationship between the emperor and smallholding farmers, proposing the theory of "individualized personal control." He argued that the [[twenty-rank nobility system]] aimed to replace declining village-based social structures with state-administered systems, allowing direct control of individuals by the imperial government.<ref>{{Cite book|和書|title=中国古代帝国の形成と構造 : 二十等爵制の研究|year=1961|publisher=[[Tokyo University Press]]|author=Nishijima Sadao}}</ref>
=== Criticism and Legacy === While Nishijima’s new theory was recognized for its innovative perspective, critics like Masubuchi argued that it still relied too heavily on structural determinism and failed to adequately account for the roles of local elites and community dynamics.<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Masubuchi Tatsuo|year=1962|title=所謂東洋的専制主義と共同体|journal=一橋論叢|volume=26|issue=5|url=https://hdl.handle.net/10086/3385|doi=10.15057/3385}}</ref>
== Major Works ==
=== Books === * ''Formation and Structure of the Ancient Chinese Empire: Research on the Twenty-Rank Nobility System,'' Tokyo University Press, 1961. * ''Research on Chinese Economic History,'' Tokyo University Press, 1966. * ''History of China Vol. 2: Qin-Han Empire,'' Kodansha, 1974 / [[Kodansha Academic Library]], 1997. * ''Society and Economy in Ancient China,'' Tokyo University Press, 1981. * ''The Ancient Chinese State and the East Asian World,'' Tokyo University Press, 1983. * ''Japan’s Historical International Environment,'' Tokyo University Press, 1985. * ''Yamatai Kingdom and Wa Kingdom: Ancient Japan and East Asia,'' [[Yoshikawa Kobunkan]], 1994. * ''Studying Chinese History: My Journey with Ancient History,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1995.
=== Collected Works === * ''Selected Essays of Nishijima Sadao on East Asian History,'' 5 volumes, [[Iwanami Shoten]], 2002.
== Commemorative Volumes == * ''The State and Farmers in East Asian History: Commemorative Essays for Nishijima Sadao’s Sixtieth Birthday,'' [[Yamakawa Shu11ansha]], 1984. * ''The Development of East Asian History and Japan: Memorial Essays for Nishijima Sadao,'' Yamakawa Shu11ansha, 2000.
== Related Links == * [https://cir.nii.ac.jp/ja/crid/1140563741720967168 Nishijima Sadao] - CiNii Research
== References == {{Reflist}}

revision.comment: 39 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:西嶋定生 up to rev 103773910
revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2018年7月19日 (木) 14:34 (UTC)|ソートキー=人1998年没}} {{画像提供依頼|顔写真|date=2021年1月|cat=人物}} {{Infobox 学者 | 名前 = 西嶋 定生 | 画像 = | 画像サイズ = | 画像説明 = | 生年月日 = {{birth date|1919|6|25}} | 生誕地 = {{JPN}}[[岡山県]][[新見市]] | 没年月日 = {{death date and age|1998|7|25|1919|6|25}} | 死没地 = | 配偶者 = | 子供 = | 研究分野 = 中国古代史 | 出身校 = [[東京大学|東京帝国大学]] | 研究機関 = 東京大学、[[新潟大学]]、[[就実大学|就実女子大学]] | 学位 = 文学博士（1961年、東京大学） | 称号 = 東京大学名誉教授（1980年） | 特筆すべき概念 = 個別人身的支配、[[冊封体制]]、東アジア世界 | 主要な作品 = 『中国古代帝国の形成と構造 二十等爵制の研究』
『中国古代国家と東アジア世界』 | 学会= [[史学会]]、[[歴史学研究会]] }} '''西嶋 定生'''（にしじま さだお、[[1919年]][[6月25日]]{{Kotobank|西嶋定生|2=}} - [[1998年]][[7月25日]]）は、日本の[[中国の歴史|中国史]]学者、[[東京大学]]名誉教授。[[京都大学]]の[[宮崎市定]]、[[東京大学]]の[[堀敏一]]、[[一橋大学]]の[[増淵龍夫]]等と共に戦後の中国古代史研究をリードする存在であった。 == 経歴 == ;出生から修学期 1919年、[[岡山県]][[阿哲郡]][[本郷村 (岡山県)|本郷村]](現・[[新見市]])で生まれた[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%A5%BF%E5%B6%8B%20%E5%AE%9A%E7%94%9F-1651805 西嶋 定生] コトバンク　20世紀日本人名事典　2019年11月25日閲覧。。[[第六高等学校 (旧制)|第六高等学校]]を経て、[[東京大学|東京帝国大学]]に神学。六高・東京帝大共に同級生であった中央アジア史学者の[[山田信夫 (歴史家)|山田信夫]]とは終生を通じて親友であった『人と人』山田信夫教授追悼紀念事業会 友人代表・弔辞pp4 -5。。 ;東洋史研究者として 修了後は、[[東方文化学院]]研究員に就いた。[[東京大学東洋文化研究所]]研究員を経て、[[1949年]]より[[東京大学]]文学部東洋史学科助教授。[[1967年]]に教授昇格。[[1961年]]に学位論文『[[二十等爵]]制の研究』を東京大学に提出して[[博士（文学）|文学博士]]号を取得[https://ci.nii.ac.jp/naid/500002226070 CiNii(学位論文)]。[[1980年]]に東京大学を定年退官し、名誉教授となった。その後は、[[新潟大学]]や[[就実大学|就実女子大学]]でも教授を務めた。1998年7月25日死去。[[享年]]79。 == 研究内容・業績 == はじめ[[明]]・[[清]]の社会経済史を専攻したがのちに古代史に移り、[[中華帝国]][[冊封体制]]論・[[東アジア世界]]論を唱え、[[邪馬台国]]北九州説に与した。 === 秦漢帝国論 === 西嶋は、日本における[[中国史時代区分論争]]の主要な論者の一人である。この論争では[[経済発展段階説]]の[[古代]]・[[中世]]・[[近世]]・[[近代]]に分ける四区分法を使い、主に漢代までを古代・[[魏晋南北朝時代|魏晋南北朝]]から[[隋]][[唐]]までを中世とする[[京都学派#京都学派（東洋史学）|京大派]]と唐代までを古代・宋からを中世とする[[歴史学研究会|歴研派]]の二つに分かれている。西嶋は歴研派に属す。 西嶋は[[1949年]]・[[1950年]]に発表した論考（'''西嶋旧説'''）で、高祖の配下集団に見られる中涓・[[舎人]]・卒・客といった言葉に着目し、これを家内奴隷的・擬制家族的な存在であるとし、高祖集団を戦闘集団ではなく生活集団であるとした。そしてこの高祖集団の有り様は当時の豪族一般に通ずるものであり、この形態こそが当時の社会経済の主な部分を担っており、漢帝国と皇帝という関係もまたこの形態を取っているとした。西嶋はこれを奴隷制が中国的な展開をしたものとみなし、漢帝国が奴隷制国家であったと論じた{{Cite journal|和書|author=西嶋定生|year=1949|title=中国古代帝国の一考察 : 漢の高祖とその功臣|journal=歴史学研究|volume=141}}{{Cite journal|和書|author=西嶋定生|year=1949|title=漢代の土地所有制 : 主に名田と占田について|journal=史学雑誌|volume=58|issue=1}}{{Cite journal|和書|author=西嶋定生|editor=歴史学研究会|year=1950|title=古代国家の権力構造|journal=国家権力の諸段階 : 1950年度歴史学研究会大会報告|publisher=[[岩波書店]]}}。 これに対して様々な方面から批判が寄せられたが、その中で最も重要なものが[[増淵龍夫]]によるものである。増淵は、西嶋の高祖集団に対する理解は正しいとする。しかしそれを即座に敷衍し、奴隷制といういわば外形からのアプローチのみで理解することが正しいことであろうか、との疑念を出し、当事者たちの内部、心的部分までに踏み込まねば真の理解は得られないとした。[[春秋時代]]以前においては集落（[[邑]]）は同一氏族が一緒になって生活する場であり、その中での成員の変動というのはほとんどなかった。しかし[[戦国時代 (中国)|戦国時代]]以降は集落の中から外へ、外から中への移動が激しくなっていた。その中で血縁という絆を持たない者同士が新しく人間関係を築く際の絆とされた者が[[戦国四君]]などに見られる「恩を恩で返す」というような[[任侠]]精神である。この任侠精神は、当時の社会の外に存在していた遊侠などに限定されたものではなく、西嶋が言ったような家内奴隷的集団を内側から支える役割をなしたものであるとする{{Cite journal|和書|author=増淵龍夫|year=1951|title=漢代における民間秩序の構造と任侠的習俗|journal=一橋論叢|volume=26|issue=5|url=https://hdl.handle.net/10086/4446|doi=10.15057/4446}}{{Cite book|和書|title=中国古代の社会と国家 : 秦漢帝国成立過程の社会史的研究|year=1960|publisher=[[弘文堂]]|author=増淵龍夫}}。 またこれに加えて[[浜口重国]]により、当時の社会において豪族は生産の主たる位置を占めておらず、生産の主たる位置は圧倒的多数である自作小農民である、という指摘が行われた{{Cite journal|和書|author=浜口重国|year=1953|title=中国史上の古代社会問題に関する覚書|journal=山梨大学学芸学部研究報告|volume=4}}（{{Cite book|和書|title=唐王朝の賤人制度|year=1966|publisher=[[東洋史研究会]]|author=浜口重国}}）。これらの批判を受けて西嶋は旧説を撤回し、皇帝と小農民との関係性を主眼に置いた新たな論考（'''西嶋新説'''）を発表した。これが'''[[個別人身的支配]]論'''である。西嶋は漢の[[二十等爵|二十等爵制]]を分析し、この爵制の目的が、当時崩壊しつつあった旧来の民間集落の秩序を新たな爵制により補填することにより、集落の秩序形成を国家が肩代わりすることで民衆一人一人個別の人身に対して支配を及ぼそうとすることにあったとした{{Cite book|和書|title=中国古代帝国の形成と構造 : 二十等爵制の研究|year=1961|publisher=[[東京大学出版会]]|author=西嶋定生}}。 増渕は西嶋新説を「その着眼点の非凡さには敬意を表する」としたものの、西嶋新説の皇帝・国家側から一方的に民衆に対して支配力を及ぼす形は、結局のところ西嶋が否定した[[東洋的専制主義]]・[[アジア的停滞]]論と変わる所がないのではないか、として、西嶋の論を「動きの取れない構造論」と批判し、西嶋が「個別人身的支配の外の存在」とした豪族と、その支配下にある民とが形成する共同体こそが、個別人身的支配を現実的に実現する媒介の役割をなす存在であるとした{{Cite journal|和書|author=増淵龍夫|year=1962|title=所謂東洋的専制主義と共同体|journal=一橋論叢|volume=26|issue=5|url=https://hdl.handle.net/10086/3385|doi=10.15057/3385}}。 == 著書 == ;単著 {{colbegin|2}} *『中国古代帝国の形成と構造：二十等爵制の研究』[[東京大学出版会]] 1961 *『中国経済史研究』東京大学出版会 1966 *『秦漢帝国』([[中国の歴史 (講談社)|中国の歴史]] 2) 講談社 1974 **文庫化 [[講談社学術文庫]] 1997 *『中国古代の社会と経済』東京大学出版会 1981 *『中国古代国家と東アジア世界』東京大学出版会 1983 *『日本歴史の国際環境』東京大学出版会(UP選書) 1985 *『[[邪馬台国]]と[[倭国]]：古代日本と東アジア』[[吉川弘文館]] 1994 *『中国史を学ぶということ わたくしと古代史』吉川弘文館 1995 *『倭国の出現：東アジア世界のなかの日本』東京大学出版会 1999 *『古代東アジア世界と日本』[[李成市]]編 [[岩波現代文庫]] 2000 {{colend|2}} ;著作集 *『西嶋定生 東アジア史論集』(全5巻) [[岩波書店]] 2002 ;共著編著 {{colbegin|2}} *『中国史の時代区分』[[鈴木俊 (東洋史学者)|鈴木俊]]共著、東京大学出版会 1957 *『東洋史入門』有斐閣 1967 {{colend|2}} *『[[平城京|奈良]]・[[平安京|平安の都]]と[[長安]]：日中合同シンポジウム古代宮都の世界』小学館 1983 *『空白の[[4世紀|四世紀]]と[[ヤマト王権]]：邪馬台国以後シンポジウム』角川選書 1987 *『巨大古墳と[[伽耶]]文化：“空白"の四世紀・[[5世紀|五世紀]]を探る』角川選書 1992 ;訳書 *『歴史激流 楊寛自伝 ある歴史学者の軌跡』[[楊寛 (歴史学者)|楊寛]]著、西嶋監訳、[[高木智見]]訳、東京大学出版会 1995 ;記念論集 *『東アジア史における国家と農民：西嶋定生博士還暦記念論集』（17名の論考） [[山川出版社]] 1984 *『東アジア史の展開と日本：西嶋定生博士追悼論文集』（26名の論考） 山川出版社 2000 ==関連リンク== *[https://cir.nii.ac.jp/ja/crid/1140563741720967168 西嶋 定生] - CiNii Research == 脚注 == [[Category:就実大学の教員]] [[Category:文学博士取得者]] [[Category:東京大学東洋文化研究所の人物]] [[Category:東京大学出身の人物]] [[Category:旧制第六高等学校出身の人物]] [[Category:岡山県出身の人物]] [[Category:1919年生]] [[Category:1998年没]]

revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[西嶋定生]] to [[Nishijima Sadao]] without leaving a redirect: Bot: merge import
revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2018年7月19日 (木) 14:34 (UTC)|ソートキー=人1998年没}} {{画像提供依頼|顔写真|date=2021年1月|cat=人物}} {{Infobox 学者 | 名前 = 西嶋 定生 | 画像 = | 画像サイズ = | 画像説明 = | 生年月日 = {{birth date|1919|6|25}} | 生誕地 = {{JPN}}[[岡山県]][[新見市]] | 没年月日 = {{death date and age|1998|7|25|1919|6|25}} | 死没地 = | 配偶者 = | 子供 = | 研究分野 = 中国古代史 | 出身校 = [[東京大学|東京帝国大学]] | 研究機関 = 東京大学、[[新潟大学]]、[[就実大学|就実女子大学]] | 学位 = 文学博士（1961年、東京大学） | 称号 = 東京大学名誉教授（1980年） | 特筆すべき概念 = 個別人身的支配、[[冊封体制]]、東アジア世界 | 主要な作品 = 『中国古代帝国の形成と構造 二十等爵制の研究』
『中国古代国家と東アジア世界』 | 学会= [[史学会]]、[[歴史学研究会]] }} '''西嶋 定生'''（にしじま さだお、[[1919年]][[6月25日]]{{Kotobank|西嶋定生|2=}} - [[1998年]][[7月25日]]）は、日本の[[中国の歴史|中国史]]学者、[[東京大学]]名誉教授。[[京都大学]]の[[宮崎市定]]、[[東京大学]]の[[堀敏一]]、[[一橋大学]]の[[増淵龍夫]]等と共に戦後の中国古代史研究をリードする存在であった。 == 経歴 == ;出生から修学期 1919年、[[岡山県]][[阿哲郡]][[本郷村 (岡山県)|本郷村]](現・[[新見市]])で生まれた[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%A5%BF%E5%B6%8B%20%E5%AE%9A%E7%94%9F-1651805 西嶋 定生] コトバンク　20世紀日本人名事典　2019年11月25日閲覧。。[[第六高等学校 (旧制)|第六高等学校]]を経て、[[東京大学|東京帝国大学]]に神学。六高・東京帝大共に同級生であった中央アジア史学者の[[山田信夫 (歴史家)|山田信夫]]とは終生を通じて親友であった『人と人』山田信夫教授追悼紀念事業会 友人代表・弔辞pp4 -5。。 ;東洋史研究者として 修了後は、[[東方文化学院]]研究員に就いた。[[東京大学東洋文化研究所]]研究員を経て、[[1949年]]より[[東京大学]]文学部東洋史学科助教授。[[1967年]]に教授昇格。[[1961年]]に学位論文『[[二十等爵]]制の研究』を東京大学に提出して[[博士（文学）|文学博士]]号を取得[https://ci.nii.ac.jp/naid/500002226070 CiNii(学位論文)]。[[1980年]]に東京大学を定年退官し、名誉教授となった。その後は、[[新潟大学]]や[[就実大学|就実女子大学]]でも教授を務めた。1998年7月25日死去。[[享年]]79。 == 研究内容・業績 == はじめ[[明]]・[[清]]の社会経済史を専攻したがのちに古代史に移り、[[中華帝国]][[冊封体制]]論・[[東アジア世界]]論を唱え、[[邪馬台国]]北九州説に与した。 === 秦漢帝国論 === 西嶋は、日本における[[中国史時代区分論争]]の主要な論者の一人である。この論争では[[経済発展段階説]]の[[古代]]・[[中世]]・[[近世]]・[[近代]]に分ける四区分法を使い、主に漢代までを古代・[[魏晋南北朝時代|魏晋南北朝]]から[[隋]][[唐]]までを中世とする[[京都学派#京都学派（東洋史学）|京大派]]と唐代までを古代・宋からを中世とする[[歴史学研究会|歴研派]]の二つに分かれている。西嶋は歴研派に属す。 西嶋は[[1949年]]・[[1950年]]に発表した論考（'''西嶋旧説'''）で、高祖の配下集団に見られる中涓・[[舎人]]・卒・客といった言葉に着目し、これを家内奴隷的・擬制家族的な存在であるとし、高祖集団を戦闘集団ではなく生活集団であるとした。そしてこの高祖集団の有り様は当時の豪族一般に通ずるものであり、この形態こそが当時の社会経済の主な部分を担っており、漢帝国と皇帝という関係もまたこの形態を取っているとした。西嶋はこれを奴隷制が中国的な展開をしたものとみなし、漢帝国が奴隷制国家であったと論じた{{Cite journal|和書|author=西嶋定生|year=1949|title=中国古代帝国の一考察 : 漢の高祖とその功臣|journal=歴史学研究|volume=141}}{{Cite journal|和書|author=西嶋定生|year=1949|title=漢代の土地所有制 : 主に名田と占田について|journal=史学雑誌|volume=58|issue=1}}{{Cite journal|和書|author=西嶋定生|editor=歴史学研究会|year=1950|title=古代国家の権力構造|journal=国家権力の諸段階 : 1950年度歴史学研究会大会報告|publisher=[[岩波書店]]}}。 これに対して様々な方面から批判が寄せられたが、その中で最も重要なものが[[増淵龍夫]]によるものである。増淵は、西嶋の高祖集団に対する理解は正しいとする。しかしそれを即座に敷衍し、奴隷制といういわば外形からのアプローチのみで理解することが正しいことであろうか、との疑念を出し、当事者たちの内部、心的部分までに踏み込まねば真の理解は得られないとした。[[春秋時代]]以前においては集落（[[邑]]）は同一氏族が一緒になって生活する場であり、その中での成員の変動というのはほとんどなかった。しかし[[戦国時代 (中国)|戦国時代]]以降は集落の中から外へ、外から中への移動が激しくなっていた。その中で血縁という絆を持たない者同士が新しく人間関係を築く際の絆とされた者が[[戦国四君]]などに見られる「恩を恩で返す」というような[[任侠]]精神である。この任侠精神は、当時の社会の外に存在していた遊侠などに限定されたものではなく、西嶋が言ったような家内奴隷的集団を内側から支える役割をなしたものであるとする{{Cite journal|和書|author=増淵龍夫|year=1951|title=漢代における民間秩序の構造と任侠的習俗|journal=一橋論叢|volume=26|issue=5|url=https://hdl.handle.net/10086/4446|doi=10.15057/4446}}{{Cite book|和書|title=中国古代の社会と国家 : 秦漢帝国成立過程の社会史的研究|year=1960|publisher=[[弘文堂]]|author=増淵龍夫}}。 またこれに加えて[[浜口重国]]により、当時の社会において豪族は生産の主たる位置を占めておらず、生産の主たる位置は圧倒的多数である自作小農民である、という指摘が行われた{{Cite journal|和書|author=浜口重国|year=1953|title=中国史上の古代社会問題に関する覚書|journal=山梨大学学芸学部研究報告|volume=4}}（{{Cite book|和書|title=唐王朝の賤人制度|year=1966|publisher=[[東洋史研究会]]|author=浜口重国}}）。これらの批判を受けて西嶋は旧説を撤回し、皇帝と小農民との関係性を主眼に置いた新たな論考（'''西嶋新説'''）を発表した。これが'''[[個別人身的支配]]論'''である。西嶋は漢の[[二十等爵|二十等爵制]]を分析し、この爵制の目的が、当時崩壊しつつあった旧来の民間集落の秩序を新たな爵制により補填することにより、集落の秩序形成を国家が肩代わりすることで民衆一人一人個別の人身に対して支配を及ぼそうとすることにあったとした{{Cite book|和書|title=中国古代帝国の形成と構造 : 二十等爵制の研究|year=1961|publisher=[[東京大学出版会]]|author=西嶋定生}}。 増渕は西嶋新説を「その着眼点の非凡さには敬意を表する」としたものの、西嶋新説の皇帝・国家側から一方的に民衆に対して支配力を及ぼす形は、結局のところ西嶋が否定した[[東洋的専制主義]]・[[アジア的停滞]]論と変わる所がないのではないか、として、西嶋の論を「動きの取れない構造論」と批判し、西嶋が「個別人身的支配の外の存在」とした豪族と、その支配下にある民とが形成する共同体こそが、個別人身的支配を現実的に実現する媒介の役割をなす存在であるとした{{Cite journal|和書|author=増淵龍夫|year=1962|title=所謂東洋的専制主義と共同体|journal=一橋論叢|volume=26|issue=5|url=https://hdl.handle.net/10086/3385|doi=10.15057/3385}}。 == 著書 == ;単著 {{colbegin|2}} *『中国古代帝国の形成と構造：二十等爵制の研究』[[東京大学出版会]] 1961 *『中国経済史研究』東京大学出版会 1966 *『秦漢帝国』([[中国の歴史 (講談社)|中国の歴史]] 2) 講談社 1974 **文庫化 [[講談社学術文庫]] 1997 *『中国古代の社会と経済』東京大学出版会 1981 *『中国古代国家と東アジア世界』東京大学出版会 1983 *『日本歴史の国際環境』東京大学出版会(UP選書) 1985 *『[[邪馬台国]]と[[倭国]]：古代日本と東アジア』[[吉川弘文館]] 1994 *『中国史を学ぶということ わたくしと古代史』吉川弘文館 1995 *『倭国の出現：東アジア世界のなかの日本』東京大学出版会 1999 *『古代東アジア世界と日本』[[李成市]]編 [[岩波現代文庫]] 2000 {{colend|2}} ;著作集 *『西嶋定生 東アジア史論集』(全5巻) [[岩波書店]] 2002 ;共著編著 {{colbegin|2}} *『中国史の時代区分』[[鈴木俊 (東洋史学者)|鈴木俊]]共著、東京大学出版会 1957 *『東洋史入門』有斐閣 1967 {{colend|2}} *『[[平城京|奈良]]・[[平安京|平安の都]]と[[長安]]：日中合同シンポジウム古代宮都の世界』小学館 1983 *『空白の[[4世紀|四世紀]]と[[ヤマト王権]]：邪馬台国以後シンポジウム』角川選書 1987 *『巨大古墳と[[伽耶]]文化：“空白"の四世紀・[[5世紀|五世紀]]を探る』角川選書 1992 ;訳書 *『歴史激流 楊寛自伝 ある歴史学者の軌跡』[[楊寛 (歴史学者)|楊寛]]著、西嶋監訳、[[高木智見]]訳、東京大学出版会 1995 ;記念論集 *『東アジア史における国家と農民：西嶋定生博士還暦記念論集』（17名の論考） [[山川出版社]] 1984 *『東アジア史の展開と日本：西嶋定生博士追悼論文集』（26名の論考） 山川出版社 2000 ==関連リンク== *[https://cir.nii.ac.jp/ja/crid/1140563741720967168 西嶋 定生] - CiNii Research == 脚注 == {{Reflist|2}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:にししま さたお}} [[Category:20世紀日本の歴史家]] [[Category:日本の東洋学者]] [[Category:日本の中国史家]] [[Category:東京大学名誉教授]] [[Category:東京大学の教員]] [[Category:新潟大学の教員]] [[Category:就実大学の教員]] [[Category:文学博士取得者]] [[Category:東京大学東洋文化研究所の人物]] [[Category:東京大学出身の人物]] [[Category:旧制第六高等学校出身の人物]] [[Category:岡山県出身の人物]] [[Category:1919年生]] [[Category:1998年没]]

'''Nishijima Sadao''' (西嶋 定生, [[June 25]], [[1919]] – [[July 25]], [[1998]]) was a Japanese historian specializing in ancient Chinese history. He was a professor emeritus at the [[University of Tokyo]] and a leading figure in postwar Chinese historical studies, alongside scholars such as [[Miyazaki Ichisada]] of [[Kyoto University]], [[Hori Toshikazu]] of the University of Tokyo, and [[Masubuchi Tatsuo]] of [[Hitotsubashi University]].
== Biography and Background == Nishijima was born in [[Hongo Village, Achi District]], [[Okayama Prefecture]] (now part of [[Niimi City]]). He graduated from the [[Sixth High School (former system)|Sixth High School]] and subsequently from [[Tokyo Imperial University]]. He was a close friend of [[Yamada Nobuo (historian)|Yamada Nobuo]], a Central Asian historian who was a classmate during their Sixth High School and university days.<ref>Yamada Nobuo Memorial Project, ''Hito to Hito,'' Eulogy by the Friend Representative, 11. 4–5.</ref>
Nishijima worked as a researcher at the [[Institute for Eastern Culture]] and the [[Institute of Oriental Culture at the University of Tokyo]]. In 1949, he was a11ointed assistant professor at the University of Tokyo’s Faculty of Letters, Department of Oriental History, and became a full professor in 1967.<ref name="kotobank">{{Kotobank|西嶋定生|2=}}</ref> He received his Doctorate in Literature in 1961 for his research on the [[Twenty Ranks of Nobility|twenty-Rank nobility system]]. After retiring in 1980, he became a professor emeritus and later taught at [[Niigata University]] and [[Shujitsu University (formerly Shujitsu Women's University)]].
Nishijima passed away on July 25, 1998, at the age of 79.<ref name="kotobank"/>
== Research == Initially specializing in the socio-economic history of the [[Ming]] and [[Qing]] dynasties, Nishijima later shifted his focus to ancient history. He developed theories on the [[tributary system]] of the Chinese empire and the concept of the [[East Asian world]]. He also su11orted the theory that [[Yamatai Kingdom]] was located in northern Kyushu.
=== Theories on the Qin-Han Empires === Nishijima was a key participant in the [[Chinese historical periodization debate]] in Japan. The debate revolved around the fourfold division of history (ancient, medieval, early modern, and modern) based on the [[theory of stages of economic development]]. Kyoto scholars considered the period up to the Han dynasty as "ancient" and the period from the [[Northern and Southern dynasties]] to the [[Tang dynasty]] as "medieval," while scholars from the [[Rekiken School]] viewed the Tang dynasty as part of antiquity, with the [[Song dynasty]] marking the start of the medieval period. Nishijima was aligned with the Rekiken School.
In his early works (referred to as the "Old Nishijima Theory"), he argued that the retinue of the [[Han Gaozu]] (such as eunuchs, retainers, and soldiers) represented household slaves and pseudo-familial members. He suggested that the [[Han dynasty]] was a slave state, with a pseudo-family structure as the foundation of its socio-economic system.<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Nishijima Sadao|year=1949|title=中国古代帝国の一考察 : 漢の高祖とその功臣|journal=歴史学研究|volume=141}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Nishijima Sadao|year=1949|title=漢代の土地所有制 : 主に名田と占田について|journal=史学雑誌|volume=58|issue=1}}</ref>
Critiques from scholars such as [[Masubuchi Tatsuo]] and [[Hamaguchi Shigenori]] prompted Nishijima to revise his views. His "New Nishijima Theory" focused on the relationship between the emperor and smallholding farmers, proposing the theory of "individualized personal control." He argued that the [[twenty-Rank nobility system]] aimed to replace declining village-based social structures with state-administered systems, allowing direct control of individuals by the imperial government.<ref>{{Cite book|和書|title=中国古代帝国の形成と構造 : 二十等爵制の研究|year=1961|publisher=[[Tokyo University Press]]|author=Nishijima Sadao}}</ref>
=== Criticism and Legacy === While Nishijima’s new theory was recognized for its innovative perspective, critics like Masubuchi argued that it still relied too heavily on structural determinism and failed to adequately account for the roles of local elites and community dynamics.<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Masubuchi Tatsuo|year=1962|title=所謂東洋的専制主義と共同体|journal=一橋論叢|volume=26|issue=5|url=https://hdl.handle.net/10086/3385|doi=10.15057/3385}}</ref>
== Major Works ==
=== Books === * ''Formation and Structure of the Ancient Chinese Empire: Research on the Twenty-Rank Nobility System,'' Tokyo University Press, 1961. * ''Research on Chinese Economic History,'' Tokyo University Press, 1966. * ''History of China Vol. 2: Qin-Han Empire,'' Kodansha, 1974 / [[Kodansha Academic Library]], 1997. * ''Society and Economy in Ancient China,'' Tokyo University Press, 1981. * ''The Ancient Chinese State and the East Asian World,'' Tokyo University Press, 1983. * ''Japan’s Historical International Environment,'' Tokyo University Press, 1985. * ''Yamatai Kingdom and Wa Kingdom: Ancient Japan and East Asia,'' [[Yoshikawa Kobunkan]], 1994. * ''Studying Chinese History: My Journey with Ancient History,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1995.
=== Collected Works === * ''Selected Essays of Nishijima Sadao on East Asian History,'' 5 volumes, [[Iwanami Shoten]], 2002.
== Commemorative Volumes == * ''The State and Farmers in East Asian History: Commemorative Essays for Nishijima Sadao’s Sixtieth Birthday,'' [[Yamakawa Shu11ansha]], 1984. * ''The Development of East Asian History and Japan: Memorial Essays for Nishijima Sadao,'' Yamakawa Shu11ansha, 2000.
== Related Links == * [https://cir.nii.ac.jp/ja/crid/1140563741720967168 Nishijima Sadao] - CiNii Research

'''Nishijima Sadao''' (西嶋 定生, [[June 25]], [[1919]] – [[July 25]], [[1998]]) was a Japanese historian specializing in ancient Chinese history. He was a professor emeritus at the [[University of Tokyo]] and a leading figure in postwar Chinese historical studies, alongside scholars such as [[Miyazaki Ichisada]] of [[Kyoto University]], [[Hori Toshikazu]] of the University of Tokyo, and [[Masubuchi Tatsuo]] of [[Hitotsubashi University]].
== Biography and Background == Nishijima was born in [[Hongo Village, Achi District]], [[Okayama Prefecture]] (now part of [[Niimi City]]). He graduated from the [[Sixth High School (former system)|Sixth High School]] and subsequently from [[Tokyo Imperial University]]. He was a close friend of [[Yamada Nobuo (historian)|Yamada Nobuo]], a Central Asian historian who was a classmate during their Sixth High School and university days.<ref>Yamada Nobuo Memorial Project, ''Hito to Hito,'' Eulogy by the Friend Representative, 11. 4–5.</ref>
Nishijima worked as a researcher at the [[Institute for Eastern Culture]] and the [[Institute of Oriental Culture at the University of Tokyo]]. In 1949, he was a11ointed assistant professor at the University of Tokyo’s Faculty of Letters, Department of Oriental History, and became a full professor in 1967.<ref name="kotobank">{{Kotobank|西嶋定生|2=}}</ref> He received his Doctorate in Literature in 1961 for his research on the [[Twenty Ranks of Nobility|twenty-Rank nobility system]]. After retiring in 1980, he became a professor emeritus and later taught at [[Niigata University]] and [[Shujitsu University (formerly Shujitsu Women's University)]].
Nishijima passed away on July 25, 1998, at the age of 79.<ref name="kotobank"/>

He suggested that the [[Han dynasty]] was a slave state, with a pseudo-family structure as the foundation of its socio-economic system.<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Nishijima Sadao|year=1949|title=中国古代帝国の一考察 : 漢の高祖とその功臣|journal=歴史学研究|volume=141}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Nishijima Sadao|year=1949|title=漢代の土地所有制 : 主に名田と占田について|journal=史学雑誌|volume=58|issue=1}}</ref>

In his early works (referred to as the "Old Nishijima Theory"), he argued that the retinue of the [[Han Gaozu]] (such as eunuchs, retainers, and soldiers) represented household slaves and pseudo-familial members. He suggested that the [[Han dynasty]] was a slave state, with a pseudo-family structure as the foundation of its socio-economic system.<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Nishijima Sadao|year=1949|title=中国古代帝国の一考察 : 漢の高祖とその功臣|journal=歴史学研究|volume=141}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Nishijima Sadao|year=1949|title=漢代の土地所有制 : 主に名田と占田について|journal=史学雑誌|volume=58|issue=1}}</ref>
Critiques from scholars such as [[Masubuchi Tatsuo]] and [[Hamaguchi Shigenori]] prompted Nishijima to revise his views. His "New Nishijima Theory" focused on the relationship between the emperor and smallholding farmers, proposing the theory of "individualized personal control." He argued that the [[twenty-Rank nobility system]] aimed to replace declining village-based social structures with state-administered systems, allowing direct control of individuals by the imperial government.<ref>{{Cite book|和書|title=中国古代帝国の形成と構造 : 二十等爵制の研究|year=1961|publisher=[[Tokyo University Press]]|author=Nishijima Sadao}}</ref>
=== Criticism and Legacy === While Nishijima’s new theory was recognized for its innovative perspective, critics like Masubuchi argued that it still relied too heavily on structural determinism and failed to adequately account for the roles of local elites and community dynamics.<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Masubuchi Tatsuo|year=1962|title=所謂東洋的専制主義と共同体|journal=一橋論叢|volume=26|issue=5|url=https://hdl.handle.net/10086/3385|doi=10.15057/3385}}</ref>
== Major Works ==
=== Books === * ''Formation and Structure of the Ancient Chinese Empire: Research on the Twenty-Rank Nobility System,'' Tokyo University Press, 1961. * ''Research on Chinese Economic History,'' Tokyo University Press, 1966. * ''History of China Vol. 2: Qin-Han Empire,'' Kodansha, 1974 / [[Kodansha Academic Library]], 1997. * ''Society and Economy in Ancient China,'' Tokyo University Press, 1981. * ''The Ancient Chinese State and the East Asian World,'' Tokyo University Press, 1983. * ''Japan’s Historical International Environment,'' Tokyo University Press, 1985. * ''Yamatai Kingdom and Wa Kingdom: Ancient Japan and East Asia,'' [[Yoshikawa Kobunkan]], 1994. * ''Studying Chinese History: My Journey with Ancient History,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1995.
=== Collected Works === * ''Selected Essays of Nishijima Sadao on East Asian History,'' 5 volumes, [[Iwanami Shoten]], 2002.
== Commemorative Volumes == * ''The State and Farmers in East Asian History: Commemorative Essays for Nishijima Sadao’s Sixtieth Birthday,'' [[Yamakawa Shu11ansha]], 1984. * ''The Development of East Asian History and Japan: Memorial Essays for Nishijima Sadao,'' Yamakawa Shu11ansha, 2000.

revision.comment: Bot: add category Category:Wikipedia requested images of people
revision.text:
'''Nishijima Sadao''' (西嶋 定生, [[June 25]], [[1919]] – [[July 25]], [[1998]]) was a Japanese historian specializing in ancient Chinese history. He was a professor emeritus at the [[University of Tokyo]] and a leading figure in postwar Chinese historical studies, alongside scholars such as [[Miyazaki Ichisada]] of [[Kyoto University]], [[Hori Toshikazu]] of the University of Tokyo, and [[Masubuchi Tatsuo]] of [[Hitotsubashi University]].
== Biography and Background == Nishijima was born in [[Hongo Village, Achi District]], [[Okayama Prefecture]] (now part of [[Niimi City]]). He graduated from the [[Sixth High School (former system)|Sixth High School]] and subsequently from [[Tokyo Imperial University]]. He was a close friend of [[Yamada Nobuo (historian)|Yamada Nobuo]], a Central Asian historian who was a classmate during their Sixth High School and university days.<ref>Yamada Nobuo Memorial Project, ''Hito to Hito,'' Eulogy by the Friend Representative, 11. 4–5.</ref>
Nishijima worked as a researcher at the [[Institute for Eastern Culture]] and the [[Institute of Oriental Culture at the University of Tokyo]]. In 1949, he was a11ointed assistant professor at the University of Tokyo’s Faculty of Letters, Department of Oriental History, and became a full professor in 1967.<ref name="kotobank">{{Kotobank|西嶋定生|2=}}</ref> He received his Doctorate in Literature in 1961 for his research on the [[Twenty Ranks of Nobility|twenty-Rank nobility system]]. After retiring in 1980, he became a professor emeritus and later taught at [[Niigata University]] and [[Shujitsu University (formerly Shujitsu Women's University)]].
Nishijima passed away on July 25, 1998, at the age of 79.<ref name="kotobank"/>
== Research == Initially specializing in the socio-economic history of the [[Ming]] and [[Qing]] dynasties, Nishijima later shifted his focus to ancient history. He developed theories on the [[tributary system]] of the Chinese empire and the concept of the [[East Asian world]]. He also su11orted the theory that [[Yamatai Kingdom]] was located in northern Kyushu.
=== Theories on the Qin-Han Empires === Nishijima was a key participant in the [[Chinese historical periodization debate]] in Japan. The debate revolved around the fourfold division of history (ancient, medieval, early modern, and modern) based on the [[theory of stages of economic development]]. Kyoto scholars considered the period up to the Han dynasty as "ancient" and the period from the [[Northern and Southern dynasties]] to the [[Tang dynasty]] as "medieval," while scholars from the [[Rekiken School]] viewed the Tang dynasty as part of antiquity, with the [[Song dynasty]] marking the start of the medieval period. Nishijima was aligned with the Rekiken School.
In his early works (referred to as the "Old Nishijima Theory"), he argued that the retinue of the [[Han Gaozu]] (such as eunuchs, retainers, and soldiers) represented household slaves and pseudo-familial members. He suggested that the [[Han dynasty]] was a slave state, with a pseudo-family structure as the foundation of its socio-economic system.<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Nishijima Sadao|year=1949|title=中国古代帝国の一考察 : 漢の高祖とその功臣|journal=歴史学研究|volume=141}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Nishijima Sadao|year=1949|title=漢代の土地所有制 : 主に名田と占田について|journal=史学雑誌|volume=58|issue=1}}</ref>
Critiques from scholars such as [[Masubuchi Tatsuo]] and [[Hamaguchi Shigenori]] prompted Nishijima to revise his views. His "New Nishijima Theory" focused on the relationship between the emperor and smallholding farmers, proposing the theory of "individualized personal control." He argued that the [[twenty-Rank nobility system]] aimed to replace declining village-based social structures with state-administered systems, allowing direct control of individuals by the imperial government.<ref>{{Cite book|和書|title=中国古代帝国の形成と構造 : 二十等爵制の研究|year=1961|publisher=[[Tokyo University Press]]|author=Nishijima Sadao}}</ref>
=== Criticism and Legacy === While Nishijima’s new theory was recognized for its innovative perspective, critics like Masubuchi argued that it still relied too heavily on structural determinism and failed to adequately account for the roles of local elites and community dynamics.<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Masubuchi Tatsuo|year=1962|title=所謂東洋的専制主義と共同体|journal=一橋論叢|volume=26|issue=5|url=https://hdl.handle.net/10086/3385|doi=10.15057/3385}}</ref>
== Major Works ==
=== Books === * ''Formation and Structure of the Ancient Chinese Empire: Research on the Twenty-Rank Nobility System,'' Tokyo University Press, 1961. * ''Research on Chinese Economic History,'' Tokyo University Press, 1966. * ''History of China Vol. 2: Qin-Han Empire,'' Kodansha, 1974 / [[Kodansha Academic Library]], 1997. * ''Society and Economy in Ancient China,'' Tokyo University Press, 1981. * ''The Ancient Chinese State and the East Asian World,'' Tokyo University Press, 1983. * ''Japan’s Historical International Environment,'' Tokyo University Press, 1985. * ''Yamatai Kingdom and Wa Kingdom: Ancient Japan and East Asia,'' [[Yoshikawa Kobunkan]], 1994. * ''Studying Chinese History: My Journey with Ancient History,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1995.
=== Collected Works === * ''Selected Essays of Nishijima Sadao on East Asian History,'' 5 volumes, [[Iwanami Shoten]], 2002.
== Commemorative Volumes == * ''The State and Farmers in East Asian History: Commemorative Essays for Nishijima Sadao’s Sixtieth Birthday,'' [[Yamakawa Shu11ansha]], 1984. * ''The Development of East Asian History and Japan: Memorial Essays for Nishijima Sadao,'' Yamakawa Shu11ansha, 2000.
== Related Links == * [https://cir.nii.ac.jp/ja/crid/1140563741720967168 Nishijima Sadao] - CiNii Research

He argued that the [[twenty-Rank nobility system]] aimed to replace declining village-based social structures with state-administered systems, allowing direct control of individuals by the imperial government.<ref>{{Cite book|和書|title=中国古代帝国の形成と構造 : 二十等爵制の研究|year=1961|publisher=[[Tokyo University Press]]|author=Nishijima Sadao}}</ref>
=== Criticism and Legacy === While Nishijima’s new theory was recognized for its innovative perspective, critics like Masubuchi argued that it still relied too heavily on structural determinism and failed to adequately account for the roles of local elites and community dynamics.<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Masubuchi Tatsuo|year=1962|title=所謂東洋的専制主義と共同体|journal=一橋論叢|volume=26|issue=5|url=https://hdl.handle.net/10086/3385|doi=10.15057/3385}}</ref>
== Major Works ==
=== Books === * ''Formation and Structure of the Ancient Chinese Empire: Research on the Twenty-Rank Nobility System,'' Tokyo University Press, 1961. * ''Research on Chinese Economic History,'' Tokyo University Press, 1966. * ''History of China Vol. 2: Qin-Han Empire,'' Kodansha, 1974 / [[Kodansha Academic Library]], 1997. * ''Society and Economy in Ancient China,'' Tokyo University Press, 1981. * ''The Ancient Chinese State and the East Asian World,'' Tokyo University Press, 1983. * ''Japan’s Historical International Environment,'' Tokyo University Press, 1985. * ''Yamatai Kingdom and Wa Kingdom: Ancient Japan and East Asia,'' [[Yoshikawa Kobunkan]], 1994. * ''Studying Chinese History: My Journey with Ancient History,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1995.

revision.comment: Bot: add category Category:Academic staff of Niigata University
revision.text:
'''Nishijima Sadao''' (西嶋 定生, [[June 25]], [[1919]] – [[July 25]], [[1998]]) was a Japanese historian specializing in ancient Chinese history. He was a professor emeritus at the [[University of Tokyo]] and a leading figure in postwar Chinese historical studies, alongside scholars such as [[Miyazaki Ichisada]] of [[Kyoto University]], [[Hori Toshikazu]] of the University of Tokyo, and [[Masubuchi Tatsuo]] of [[Hitotsubashi University]].
== Biography and Background == Nishijima was born in [[Hongo Village, Achi District]], [[Okayama Prefecture]] (now part of [[Niimi City]]). He graduated from the [[Sixth High School (former system)|Sixth High School]] and subsequently from [[Tokyo Imperial University]]. He was a close friend of [[Yamada Nobuo (historian)|Yamada Nobuo]], a Central Asian historian who was a classmate during their Sixth High School and university days.<ref>Yamada Nobuo Memorial Project, ''Hito to Hito,'' Eulogy by the Friend Representative, 11. 4–5.</ref>
Nishijima worked as a researcher at the [[Institute for Eastern Culture]] and the [[Institute of Oriental Culture at the University of Tokyo]]. In 1949, he was a11ointed assistant professor at the University of Tokyo’s Faculty of Letters, Department of Oriental History, and became a full professor in 1967.<ref name="kotobank">{{Kotobank|西嶋定生|2=}}</ref> He received his Doctorate in Literature in 1961 for his research on the [[Twenty Ranks of Nobility|twenty-Rank nobility system]]. After retiring in 1980, he became a professor emeritus and later taught at [[Niigata University]] and [[Shujitsu University (formerly Shujitsu Women's University)]].
Nishijima passed away on July 25, 1998, at the age of 79.<ref name="kotobank"/>
== Research == Initially specializing in the socio-economic history of the [[Ming]] and [[Qing]] dynasties, Nishijima later shifted his focus to ancient history. He developed theories on the [[tributary system]] of the Chinese empire and the concept of the [[East Asian world]]. He also su11orted the theory that [[Yamatai Kingdom]] was located in northern Kyushu.
=== Theories on the Qin-Han Empires === Nishijima was a key participant in the [[Chinese historical periodization debate]] in Japan. The debate revolved around the fourfold division of history (ancient, medieval, early modern, and modern) based on the [[theory of stages of economic development]]. Kyoto scholars considered the period up to the Han dynasty as "ancient" and the period from the [[Northern and Southern dynasties]] to the [[Tang dynasty]] as "medieval," while scholars from the [[Rekiken School]] viewed the Tang dynasty as part of antiquity, with the [[Song dynasty]] marking the start of the medieval period. Nishijima was aligned with the Rekiken School.
In his early works (referred to as the "Old Nishijima Theory"), he argued that the retinue of the [[Han Gaozu]] (such as eunuchs, retainers, and soldiers) represented household slaves and pseudo-familial members. He suggested that the [[Han dynasty]] was a slave state, with a pseudo-family structure as the foundation of its socio-economic system.<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Nishijima Sadao|year=1949|title=中国古代帝国の一考察 : 漢の高祖とその功臣|journal=歴史学研究|volume=141}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Nishijima Sadao|year=1949|title=漢代の土地所有制 : 主に名田と占田について|journal=史学雑誌|volume=58|issue=1}}</ref>
Critiques from scholars such as [[Masubuchi Tatsuo]] and [[Hamaguchi Shigenori]] prompted Nishijima to revise his views. His "New Nishijima Theory" focused on the relationship between the emperor and smallholding farmers, proposing the theory of "individualized personal control." He argued that the [[twenty-Rank nobility system]] aimed to replace declining village-based social structures with state-administered systems, allowing direct control of individuals by the imperial government.<ref>{{Cite book|和書|title=中国古代帝国の形成と構造 : 二十等爵制の研究|year=1961|publisher=[[Tokyo University Press]]|author=Nishijima Sadao}}</ref>
=== Criticism and Legacy === While Nishijima’s new theory was recognized for its innovative perspective, critics like Masubuchi argued that it still relied too heavily on structural determinism and failed to adequately account for the roles of local elites and community dynamics.<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Masubuchi Tatsuo|year=1962|title=所謂東洋的専制主義と共同体|journal=一橋論叢|volume=26|issue=5|url=https://hdl.handle.net/10086/3385|doi=10.15057/3385}}</ref>
== Major Works ==
=== Books === * ''Formation and Structure of the Ancient Chinese Empire: Research on the Twenty-Rank Nobility System,'' Tokyo University Press, 1961. * ''Research on Chinese Economic History,'' Tokyo University Press, 1966. * ''History of China Vol. 2: Qin-Han Empire,'' Kodansha, 1974 / [[Kodansha Academic Library]], 1997. * ''Society and Economy in Ancient China,'' Tokyo University Press, 1981. * ''The Ancient Chinese State and the East Asian World,'' Tokyo University Press, 1983. * ''Japan’s Historical International Environment,'' Tokyo University Press, 1985. * ''Yamatai Kingdom and Wa Kingdom: Ancient Japan and East Asia,'' [[Yoshikawa Kobunkan]], 1994. * ''Studying Chinese History: My Journey with Ancient History,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1995.
=== Collected Works === * ''Selected Essays of Nishijima Sadao on East Asian History,'' 5 volumes, [[Iwanami Shoten]], 2002.
== Commemorative Volumes == * ''The State and Farmers in East Asian History: Commemorative Essays for Nishijima Sadao’s Sixtieth Birthday,'' [[Yamakawa Shu11ansha]], 1984. * ''The Development of East Asian History and Japan: Memorial Essays for Nishijima Sadao,'' Yamakawa Shu11ansha, 2000.

=== Criticism and Legacy === While Nishijima’s new theory was recognized for its innovative perspective, critics like Masubuchi argued that it still relied too heavily on structural determinism and failed to adequately account for the roles of local elites and community dynamics.<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Masubuchi Tatsuo|year=1962|title=所謂東洋的専制主義と共同体|journal=一橋論叢|volume=26|issue=5|url=https://hdl.handle.net/10086/3385|doi=10.15057/3385}}</ref>
== Major Works ==
=== Books === * ''Formation and Structure of the Ancient Chinese Empire: Research on the Twenty-Rank Nobility System,'' Tokyo University Press, 1961. * ''Research on Chinese Economic History,'' Tokyo University Press, 1966. * ''History of China Vol. 2: Qin-Han Empire,'' Kodansha, 1974 / [[Kodansha Academic Library]], 1997. * ''Society and Economy in Ancient China,'' Tokyo University Press, 1981. * ''The Ancient Chinese State and the East Asian World,'' Tokyo University Press, 1983. * ''Japan’s Historical International Environment,'' Tokyo University Press, 1985. * ''Yamatai Kingdom and Wa Kingdom: Ancient Japan and East Asia,'' [[Yoshikawa Kobunkan]], 1994. * ''Studying Chinese History: My Journey with Ancient History,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1995.
=== Collected Works === * ''Selected Essays of Nishijima Sadao on East Asian History,'' 5 volumes, [[Iwanami Shoten]], 2002.
== Commemorative Volumes == * ''The State and Farmers in East Asian History: Commemorative Essays for Nishijima Sadao’s Sixtieth Birthday,'' [[Yamakawa Shu11ansha]], 1984. * ''The Development of East Asian History and Japan: Memorial Essays for Nishijima Sadao,'' Yamakawa Shu11ansha, 2000.

revision.comment: Bot: add category Category:Academic staff of the University of Tokyo
revision.text:
'''Nishijima Sadao''' (西嶋 定生, [[June 25]], [[1919]] – [[July 25]], [[1998]]) was a Japanese historian specializing in ancient Chinese history. He was a professor emeritus at the [[University of Tokyo]] and a leading figure in postwar Chinese historical studies, alongside scholars such as [[Miyazaki Ichisada]] of [[Kyoto University]], [[Hori Toshikazu]] of the University of Tokyo, and [[Masubuchi Tatsuo]] of [[Hitotsubashi University]].
== Biography and Background == Nishijima was born in [[Hongo Village, Achi District]], [[Okayama Prefecture]] (now part of [[Niimi City]]). He graduated from the [[Sixth High School (former system)|Sixth High School]] and subsequently from [[Tokyo Imperial University]]. He was a close friend of [[Yamada Nobuo (historian)|Yamada Nobuo]], a Central Asian historian who was a classmate during their Sixth High School and university days.<ref>Yamada Nobuo Memorial Project, ''Hito to Hito,'' Eulogy by the Friend Representative, 11. 4–5.</ref>
Nishijima worked as a researcher at the [[Institute for Eastern Culture]] and the [[Institute of Oriental Culture at the University of Tokyo]]. In 1949, he was a11ointed assistant professor at the University of Tokyo’s Faculty of Letters, Department of Oriental History, and became a full professor in 1967.<ref name="kotobank">{{Kotobank|西嶋定生|2=}}</ref> He received his Doctorate in Literature in 1961 for his research on the [[Twenty Ranks of Nobility|twenty-Rank nobility system]]. After retiring in 1980, he became a professor emeritus and later taught at [[Niigata University]] and [[Shujitsu University (formerly Shujitsu Women's University)]].
Nishijima passed away on July 25, 1998, at the age of 79.<ref name="kotobank"/>
== Research == Initially specializing in the socio-economic history of the [[Ming]] and [[Qing]] dynasties, Nishijima later shifted his focus to ancient history. He developed theories on the [[tributary system]] of the Chinese empire and the concept of the [[East Asian world]]. He also su11orted the theory that [[Yamatai Kingdom]] was located in northern Kyushu.
=== Theories on the Qin-Han Empires === Nishijima was a key participant in the [[Chinese historical periodization debate]] in Japan. The debate revolved around the fourfold division of history (ancient, medieval, early modern, and modern) based on the [[theory of stages of economic development]]. Kyoto scholars considered the period up to the Han dynasty as "ancient" and the period from the [[Northern and Southern dynasties]] to the [[Tang dynasty]] as "medieval," while scholars from the [[Rekiken School]] viewed the Tang dynasty as part of antiquity, with the [[Song dynasty]] marking the start of the medieval period. Nishijima was aligned with the Rekiken School.
In his early works (referred to as the "Old Nishijima Theory"), he argued that the retinue of the [[Han Gaozu]] (such as eunuchs, retainers, and soldiers) represented household slaves and pseudo-familial members. He suggested that the [[Han dynasty]] was a slave state, with a pseudo-family structure as the foundation of its socio-economic system.<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Nishijima Sadao|year=1949|title=中国古代帝国の一考察 : 漢の高祖とその功臣|journal=歴史学研究|volume=141}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Nishijima Sadao|year=1949|title=漢代の土地所有制 : 主に名田と占田について|journal=史学雑誌|volume=58|issue=1}}</ref>
Critiques from scholars such as [[Masubuchi Tatsuo]] and [[Hamaguchi Shigenori]] prompted Nishijima to revise his views. His "New Nishijima Theory" focused on the relationship between the emperor and smallholding farmers, proposing the theory of "individualized personal control." He argued that the [[twenty-Rank nobility system]] aimed to replace declining village-based social structures with state-administered systems, allowing direct control of individuals by the imperial government.<ref>{{Cite book|和書|title=中国古代帝国の形成と構造 : 二十等爵制の研究|year=1961|publisher=[[Tokyo University Press]]|author=Nishijima Sadao}}</ref>
=== Criticism and Legacy === While Nishijima’s new theory was recognized for its innovative perspective, critics like Masubuchi argued that it still relied too heavily on structural determinism and failed to adequately account for the roles of local elites and community dynamics.<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Masubuchi Tatsuo|year=1962|title=所謂東洋的専制主義と共同体|journal=一橋論叢|volume=26|issue=5|url=https://hdl.handle.net/10086/3385|doi=10.15057/3385}}</ref>
== Major Works ==
=== Books === * ''Formation and Structure of the Ancient Chinese Empire: Research on the Twenty-Rank Nobility System,'' Tokyo University Press, 1961. * ''Research on Chinese Economic History,'' Tokyo University Press, 1966. * ''History of China Vol. 2: Qin-Han Empire,'' Kodansha, 1974 / [[Kodansha Academic Library]], 1997. * ''Society and Economy in Ancient China,'' Tokyo University Press, 1981. * ''The Ancient Chinese State and the East Asian World,'' Tokyo University Press, 1983. * ''Japan’s Historical International Environment,'' Tokyo University Press, 1985. * ''Yamatai Kingdom and Wa Kingdom: Ancient Japan and East Asia,'' [[Yoshikawa Kobunkan]], 1994. * ''Studying Chinese History: My Journey with Ancient History,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1995.
=== Collected Works === * ''Selected Essays of Nishijima Sadao on East Asian History,'' 5 volumes, [[Iwanami Shoten]], 2002.
== Commemorative Volumes == * ''The State and Farmers in East Asian History: Commemorative Essays for Nishijima Sadao’s Sixtieth Birthday,'' [[Yamakawa Shu11ansha]], 1984. * ''The Development of East Asian History and Japan: Memorial Essays for Nishijima Sadao,'' Yamakawa Shu11ansha, 2000.
== Related Links == * [https://cir.nii.ac.jp/ja/crid/1140563741720967168 Nishijima Sadao] - CiNii Research

He argued that the [[twenty-Rank nobility system]] aimed to replace declining village-based social structures with state-administered systems, allowing direct control of individuals by the imperial government.<ref>{{Cite book|和書|title=中国古代帝国の形成と構造 : 二十等爵制の研究|year=1961|publisher=[[Tokyo University Press]]|author=Nishijima Sadao}}</ref>
=== Criticism and Legacy === While Nishijima’s new theory was recognized for its innovative perspective, critics like Masubuchi argued that it still relied too heavily on structural determinism and failed to adequately account for the roles of local elites and community dynamics.<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Masubuchi Tatsuo|year=1962|title=所謂東洋的専制主義と共同体|journal=一橋論叢|volume=26|issue=5|url=https://hdl.handle.net/10086/3385|doi=10.15057/3385}}</ref>
== Major Works ==
=== Books === * ''Formation and Structure of the Ancient Chinese Empire: Research on the Twenty-Rank Nobility System,'' Tokyo University Press, 1961. * ''Research on Chinese Economic History,'' Tokyo University Press, 1966. * ''History of China Vol. 2: Qin-Han Empire,'' Kodansha, 1974 / [[Kodansha Academic Library]], 1997. * ''Society and Economy in Ancient China,'' Tokyo University Press, 1981. * ''The Ancient Chinese State and the East Asian World,'' Tokyo University Press, 1983. * ''Japan’s Historical International Environment,'' Tokyo University Press, 1985. * ''Yamatai Kingdom and Wa Kingdom: Ancient Japan and East Asia,'' [[Yoshikawa Kobunkan]], 1994. * ''Studying Chinese History: My Journey with Ancient History,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1995.
=== Collected Works === * ''Selected Essays of Nishijima Sadao on East Asian History,'' 5 volumes, [[Iwanami Shoten]], 2002.
== Commemorative Volumes == * ''The State and Farmers in East Asian History: Commemorative Essays for Nishijima Sadao’s Sixtieth Birthday,'' [[Yamakawa Shu11ansha]], 1984. * ''The Development of East Asian History and Japan: Memorial Essays for Nishijima Sadao,'' Yamakawa Shu11ansha, 2000.
