<!--Nishijima Sadao--><!--Nishijima Sadao--><!--Nishijima Sadao--><!--Nishijima Sadao--><!--Nishijima Sadao--><!--Nishijima Sadao--> [[Category:Wikipedia requested images of people]] [[Category:就実大学の教員]] [[Category:Births in Okayama prefecture]] [[Category:文学博士取得者]] [[Category:Academic staff of Niigata University]] [[Category:日本の中国史家]] [[Category:Japanese orientalists]] [[Category:旧制第六高等学校出身の人物]] [[Category:Academic staff of the University of Tokyo]] [[Category:東京大学東洋文化研究所の人物]]

'''Nishijima Sadao''' (西嶋 定生, [[June 25]], [[1919]] – [[July 25]], [[1998]]) was a Japanese historian specializing in ancient Chinese history. He was a professor emeritus at the [[University of Tokyo]] and a leading figure in postwar Chinese historical studies, alongside scholars such as [[Miyazaki Ichisada]] of [[Kyoto University]], [[Hori Toshikazu]] of the University of Tokyo, and [[Masubuchi Tatsuo]] of [[Hitotsubashi University]].
== Biography and Background == Nishijima was born in [[Hongo Village, Achi District]], [[Okayama Prefecture]] (now part of [[Niimi City]]). He graduated from the [[Sixth High School (former system)|Sixth High School]] and subsequently from [[Tokyo Imperial University]]. He was a close friend of [[Yamada Nobuo (historian)|Yamada Nobuo]], a Central Asian historian who was a classmate during their Sixth High School and university days.<ref>Yamada Nobuo Memorial Project, ''Hito to Hito,'' Eulogy by the Friend Representative, 11. 4–5.</ref>
Nishijima worked as a researcher at the [[Institute for Eastern Culture]] and the [[Institute of Oriental Culture at the University of Tokyo]]. In 1949, he was a11ointed assistant professor at the University of Tokyo’s Faculty of Letters, Department of Oriental History, and became a full professor in 1967.<ref name="kotobank">{{Kotobank|西嶋定生|2=}}</ref> He received his Doctorate in Literature in 1961 for his research on the [[Twenty Ranks of Nobility|twenty-Rank nobility system]]. After retiring in 1980, he became a professor emeritus and later taught at [[Niigata University]] and [[Shujitsu University (formerly Shujitsu Women's University)]].
Nishijima passed away on July 25, 1998, at the age of 79.<ref name="kotobank"/>
== Research == Initially specializing in the socio-economic history of the [[Ming]] and [[Qing]] dynasties, Nishijima later shifted his focus to ancient history. He developed theories on the [[tributary system]] of the Chinese empire and the concept of the [[East Asian world]]. He also su11orted the theory that [[Yamatai Kingdom]] was located in northern Kyushu.
=== Theories on the Qin-Han Empires === Nishijima was a key participant in the [[Chinese historical periodization debate]] in Japan. The debate revolved around the fourfold division of history (ancient, medieval, early modern, and modern) based on the [[theory of stages of economic development]]. Kyoto scholars considered the period up to the Han dynasty as "ancient" and the period from the [[Northern and Southern dynasties]] to the [[Tang dynasty]] as "medieval," while scholars from the [[Rekiken School]] viewed the Tang dynasty as part of antiquity, with the [[Song dynasty]] marking the start of the medieval period. Nishijima was aligned with the Rekiken School.
In his early works (referred to as the "Old Nishijima Theory"), he argued that the retinue of the [[Han Gaozu]] (such as eunuchs, retainers, and soldiers) represented household slaves and pseudo-familial members. He suggested that the [[Han dynasty]] was a slave state, with a pseudo-family structure as the foundation of its socio-economic system.<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Nishijima Sadao|year=1949|title=中国古代帝国の一考察 : 漢の高祖とその功臣|journal=歴史学研究|volume=141}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Nishijima Sadao|year=1949|title=漢代の土地所有制 : 主に名田と占田について|journal=史学雑誌|volume=58|issue=1}}</ref>
Critiques from scholars such as [[Masubuchi Tatsuo]] and [[Hamaguchi Shigenori]] prompted Nishijima to revise his views. His "New Nishijima Theory" focused on the relationship between the emperor and smallholding farmers, proposing the theory of "individualized personal control." He argued that the [[twenty-Rank nobility system]] aimed to replace declining village-based social structures with state-administered systems, allowing direct control of individuals by the imperial government.<ref>{{Cite book|和書|title=中国古代帝国の形成と構造 : 二十等爵制の研究|year=1961|publisher=[[Tokyo University Press]]|author=Nishijima Sadao}}</ref>
=== Criticism and Legacy === While Nishijima’s new theory was recognized for its innovative perspective, critics like Masubuchi argued that it still relied too heavily on structural determinism and failed to adequately account for the roles of local elites and community dynamics.<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Masubuchi Tatsuo|year=1962|title=所謂東洋的専制主義と共同体|journal=一橋論叢|volume=26|issue=5|url=https://hdl.handle.net/10086/3385|doi=10.15057/3385}}</ref>
== Major Works ==
=== Books === * ''Formation and Structure of the Ancient Chinese Empire: Research on the Twenty-Rank Nobility System,'' Tokyo University Press, 1961. * ''Research on Chinese Economic History,'' Tokyo University Press, 1966. * ''History of China Vol. 2: Qin-Han Empire,'' Kodansha, 1974 / [[Kodansha Academic Library]], 1997. * ''Society and Economy in Ancient China,'' Tokyo University Press, 1981. * ''The Ancient Chinese State and the East Asian World,'' Tokyo University Press, 1983. * ''Japan’s Historical International Environment,'' Tokyo University Press, 1985. * ''Yamatai Kingdom and Wa Kingdom: Ancient Japan and East Asia,'' [[Yoshikawa Kobunkan]], 1994. * ''Studying Chinese History: My Journey with Ancient History,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1995.
=== Collected Works === * ''Selected Essays of Nishijima Sadao on East Asian History,'' 5 volumes, [[Iwanami Shoten]], 2002.
== Commemorative Volumes == * ''The State and Farmers in East Asian History: Commemorative Essays for Nishijima Sadao’s Sixtieth Birthday,'' [[Yamakawa Shu11ansha]], 1984. * ''The Development of East Asian History and Japan: Memorial Essays for Nishijima Sadao,'' Yamakawa Shu11ansha, 2000.
== Related Links == * [https://cir.nii.ac.jp/ja/crid/1140563741720967168 Nishijima Sadao] - CiNii Research

He suggested that the [[Han dynasty]] was a slave state, with a pseudo-family structure as the foundation of its socio-economic system.<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Nishijima Sadao|year=1949|title=中国古代帝国の一考察 : 漢の高祖とその功臣|journal=歴史学研究|volume=141}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Nishijima Sadao|year=1949|title=漢代の土地所有制 : 主に名田と占田について|journal=史学雑誌|volume=58|issue=1}}</ref>
Critiques from scholars such as [[Masubuchi Tatsuo]] and [[Hamaguchi Shigenori]] prompted Nishijima to revise his views. His "New Nishijima Theory" focused on the relationship between the emperor and smallholding farmers, proposing the theory of "individualized personal control." He argued that the [[twenty-Rank nobility system]] aimed to replace declining village-based social structures with state-administered systems, allowing direct control of individuals by the imperial government.<ref>{{Cite book|和書|title=中国古代帝国の形成と構造 : 二十等爵制の研究|year=1961|publisher=[[Tokyo University Press]]|author=Nishijima Sadao}}</ref>
=== Criticism and Legacy === While Nishijima’s new theory was recognized for its innovative perspective, critics like Masubuchi argued that it still relied too heavily on structural determinism and failed to adequately account for the roles of local elites and community dynamics.<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Masubuchi Tatsuo|year=1962|title=所謂東洋的専制主義と共同体|journal=一橋論叢|volume=26|issue=5|url=https://hdl.handle.net/10086/3385|doi=10.15057/3385}}</ref>
== Major Works ==
=== Books === * ''Formation and Structure of the Ancient Chinese Empire: Research on the Twenty-Rank Nobility System,'' Tokyo University Press, 1961. * ''Research on Chinese Economic History,'' Tokyo University Press, 1966. * ''History of China Vol. 2: Qin-Han Empire,'' Kodansha, 1974 / [[Kodansha Academic Library]], 1997. * ''Society and Economy in Ancient China,'' Tokyo University Press, 1981. * ''The Ancient Chinese State and the East Asian World,'' Tokyo University Press, 1983. * ''Japan’s Historical International Environment,'' Tokyo University Press, 1985. * ''Yamatai Kingdom and Wa Kingdom: Ancient Japan and East Asia,'' [[Yoshikawa Kobunkan]], 1994. * ''Studying Chinese History: My Journey with Ancient History,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1995.
=== Collected Works === * ''Selected Essays of Nishijima Sadao on East Asian History,'' 5 volumes, [[Iwanami Shoten]], 2002.
== Commemorative Volumes == * ''The State and Farmers in East Asian History: Commemorative Essays for Nishijima Sadao’s Sixtieth Birthday,'' [[Yamakawa Shu11ansha]], 1984. * ''The Development of East Asian History and Japan: Memorial Essays for Nishijima Sadao,'' Yamakawa Shu11ansha, 2000.
== Related Links == * [https://cir.nii.ac.jp/ja/crid/1140563741720967168 Nishijima Sadao] - CiNii Research

revision.comment: Bot: remove deleted category Professors of Niigata University
revision.text:
'''Nishijima Sadao''' (西嶋 定生, [[June 25]], [[:en:1919|1919]] – [[July 25]], [[:en:1998|1998]]) was a Japanese historian specializing in ancient Chinese history. He was a professor emeritus at the [[University of Tokyo]] and a leading figure in postwar Chinese historical studies, alongside scholars such as [[Miyazaki Ichisada]] of [[Kyoto University]], [[Hori Toshikazu]] of the University of Tokyo, and [[Masubuchi Tatsuo]] of [[Hitotsubashi University]].
== Biography and Background == Nishijima was born in [[Hongo Village, Achi District]], [[Okayama Prefecture]] (now part of [[Niimi City]]). He graduated from the [[Sixth High School (former system)|Sixth High School]] and subsequently from [[Tokyo Imperial University]]. He was a close friend of [[Yamada Nobuo (historian)|Yamada Nobuo]], a Central Asian historian who was a classmate during their Sixth High School and university days.<ref>Yamada Nobuo Memorial Project, ''Hito to Hito,'' Eulogy by the Friend Representative, 11. 4–5.</ref>
Nishijima worked as a researcher at the [[Institute for Eastern Culture]] and the [[Institute of Oriental Culture at the University of Tokyo]]. In 1949, he was a11ointed assistant professor at the University of Tokyo’s Faculty of Letters, Department of Oriental History, and became a full professor in 1967.<ref name="kotobank">{{Kotobank|西嶋定生|2=}}</ref> He received his Doctorate in Literature in 1961 for his research on the [[Twenty Ranks of Nobility|twenty-Rank nobility system]]. After retiring in 1980, he became a professor emeritus and later taught at [[Niigata University]] and [[Shujitsu University (formerly Shujitsu Women's University)]].
Nishijima passed away on July 25, 1998, at the age of 79.<ref name="kotobank"/>
== Research == Initially specializing in the socio-economic history of the [[Ming]] and [[Qing]] dynasties, Nishijima later shifted his focus to ancient history. He developed theories on the [[tributary system]] of the Chinese empire and the concept of the [[East Asian world]]. He also su11orted the theory that [[Yamatai Kingdom]] was located in northern Kyushu.
=== Theories on the Qin-Han Empires === Nishijima was a key participant in the [[Chinese historical periodization debate]] in Japan. The debate revolved around the fourfold division of history (ancient, medieval, early modern, and modern) based on the [[theory of stages of economic development]]. Kyoto scholars considered the period up to the Han dynasty as "ancient" and the period from the [[Northern and Southern dynasties]] to the [[Tang dynasty]] as "medieval," while scholars from the [[Rekiken School]] viewed the Tang dynasty as part of antiquity, with the [[Song dynasty]] marking the start of the medieval period. Nishijima was aligned with the Rekiken School.
In his early works (referred to as the "Old Nishijima Theory"), he argued that the retinue of the [[Han Gaozu]] (such as eunuchs, retainers, and soldiers) represented household slaves and pseudo-familial members. He suggested that the [[Han dynasty]] was a slave state, with a pseudo-family structure as the foundation of its socio-economic system.<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Nishijima Sadao|year=1949|title=中国古代帝国の一考察 : 漢の高祖とその功臣|journal=歴史学研究|volume=141}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Nishijima Sadao|year=1949|title=漢代の土地所有制 : 主に名田と占田について|journal=史学雑誌|volume=58|issue=1}}</ref>
Critiques from scholars such as [[Masubuchi Tatsuo]] and [[Hamaguchi Shigenori]] prompted Nishijima to revise his views. His "New Nishijima Theory" focused on the relationship between the emperor and smallholding farmers, proposing the theory of "individualized personal control." He argued that the [[twenty-Rank nobility system]] aimed to replace declining village-based social structures with state-administered systems, allowing direct control of individuals by the imperial government.<ref>{{Cite book|和書|title=中国古代帝国の形成と構造 : 二十等爵制の研究|year=1961|publisher=[[Tokyo University Press]]|author=Nishijima Sadao}}</ref>
=== Criticism and Legacy === While Nishijima’s new theory was recognized for its innovative perspective, critics like Masubuchi argued that it still relied too heavily on structural determinism and failed to adequately account for the roles of local elites and community dynamics.<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Masubuchi Tatsuo|year=1962|title=所謂東洋的専制主義と共同体|journal=一橋論叢|volume=26|issue=5|url=https://hdl.handle.net/10086/3385|doi=10.15057/3385}}</ref>
== Major Works ==
=== Books === * ''Formation and Structure of the Ancient Chinese Empire: Research on the Twenty-Rank Nobility System,'' Tokyo University Press, 1961. * ''Research on Chinese Economic History,'' Tokyo University Press, 1966. * ''History of China Vol. 2: Qin-Han Empire,'' Kodansha, 1974 / [[Kodansha Academic Library]], 1997. * ''Society and Economy in Ancient China,'' Tokyo University Press, 1981. * ''The Ancient Chinese State and the East Asian World,'' Tokyo University Press, 1983. * ''Japan’s Historical International Environment,'' Tokyo University Press, 1985. * ''Yamatai Kingdom and Wa Kingdom: Ancient Japan and East Asia,'' [[Yoshikawa Kobunkan]], 1994. * ''Studying Chinese History: My Journey with Ancient History,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1995.
=== Collected Works === * ''Selected Essays of Nishijima Sadao on East Asian History,'' 5 volumes, [[Iwanami Shoten]], 2002.
== Commemorative Volumes == * ''The State and Farmers in East Asian History: Commemorative Essays for Nishijima Sadao’s Sixtieth Birthday,'' [[Yamakawa Shu11ansha]], 1984. * ''The Development of East Asian History and Japan: Memorial Essays for Nishijima Sadao,'' Yamakawa Shu11ansha, 2000.
== Related Links == * [https://cir.nii.ac.jp/ja/crid/1140563741720967168 Nishijima Sadao] - CiNii Research

The debate revolved around the fourfold division of history (ancient, medieval, early modern, and modern) based on the [[theory of stages of economic development]]. Kyoto scholars considered the period up to the Han dynasty as "ancient" and the period from the [[Northern and Southern dynasties]] to the [[Tang dynasty]] as "medieval," while scholars from the [[Rekiken School]] viewed the Tang dynasty as part of antiquity, with the [[Song dynasty]] marking the start of the medieval period. Nishijima was aligned with the Rekiken School.
In his early works (referred to as the "Old Nishijima Theory"), he argued that the retinue of the [[:en:Han Gaozu|Han Gaozu]] (such as eunuchs, retainers, and soldiers) represented household slaves and pseudo-familial members. He suggested that the [[Han dynasty]] was a slave state, with a pseudo-family structure as the foundation of its socio-economic system.<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Nishijima Sadao|year=1949|title=中国古代帝国の一考察 : 漢の高祖とその功臣|journal=歴史学研究|volume=141}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Nishijima Sadao|year=1949|title=漢代の土地所有制 : 主に名田と占田について|journal=史学雑誌|volume=58|issue=1}}</ref>
Critiques from scholars such as [[Masubuchi Tatsuo]] and [[Hamaguchi Shigenori]] prompted Nishijima to revise his views. His "New Nishijima Theory" focused on the relationship between the emperor and smallholding farmers, proposing the theory of "individualized personal control." He argued that the [[twenty-Rank nobility system]] aimed to replace declining village-based social structures with state-administered systems, allowing direct control of individuals by the imperial government.<ref>{{Cite book|和書|title=中国古代帝国の形成と構造 : 二十等爵制の研究|year=1961|publisher=[[Tokyo University Press]]|author=Nishijima Sadao}}</ref>
=== Criticism and Legacy === While Nishijima’s new theory was recognized for its innovative perspective, critics like Masubuchi argued that it still relied too heavily on structural determinism and failed to adequately account for the roles of local elites and community dynamics.<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Masubuchi Tatsuo|year=1962|title=所謂東洋的専制主義と共同体|journal=一橋論叢|volume=26|issue=5|url=https://hdl.handle.net/10086/3385|doi=10.15057/3385}}</ref>
== Major Works ==
=== Books === * ''Formation and Structure of the Ancient Chinese Empire: Research on the Twenty-Rank Nobility System,'' Tokyo University Press, 1961. * ''Research on Chinese Economic History,'' Tokyo University Press, 1966. * ''History of China Vol. 2: Qin-Han Empire,'' Kodansha, 1974 / [[Kodansha Academic Library]], 1997. * ''Society and Economy in Ancient China,'' Tokyo University Press, 1981. * ''The Ancient Chinese State and the East Asian World,'' Tokyo University Press, 1983. * ''Japan’s Historical International Environment,'' Tokyo University Press, 1985. * ''Yamatai Kingdom and Wa Kingdom: Ancient Japan and East Asia,'' [[Yoshikawa Kobunkan]], 1994. * ''Studying Chinese History: My Journey with Ancient History,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1995.
=== Collected Works === * ''Selected Essays of Nishijima Sadao on East Asian History,'' 5 volumes, [[Iwanami Shoten]], 2002.
== Commemorative Volumes == * ''The State and Farmers in East Asian History: Commemorative Essays for Nishijima Sadao’s Sixtieth Birthday,'' [[Yamakawa Shu11ansha]], 1984. * ''The Development of East Asian History and Japan: Memorial Essays for Nishijima Sadao,'' Yamakawa Shu11ansha, 2000.
== Related Links == * [https://cir.nii.ac.jp/ja/crid/1140563741720967168 Nishijima Sadao] - CiNii Research

revision.text:
'''Nishijima Sadao''' (西嶋 定生, [[June 25]], [[:en:1919|1919]] – [[July 25]], [[:en:1998|1998]]) was a Japanese historian specializing in ancient Chinese history. He was a professor emeritus at the [[University of Tokyo]] and a leading figure in postwar Chinese historical studies, alongside scholars such as [[Miyazaki Ichisada]] of [[Kyoto University]], [[Hori Toshikazu]] of the University of Tokyo, and [[Masubuchi Tatsuo]] of [[Hitotsubashi University]].
== Biography and Background == Nishijima was born in [[Hongo Village, Achi District]], [[Okayama Prefecture]] (now part of [[Niimi City]]). He graduated from the [[Sixth High School (former system)|Sixth High School]] and subsequently from [[Tokyo Imperial University]]. He was a close friend of [[Yamada Nobuo (historian)|Yamada Nobuo]], a Central Asian historian who was a classmate during their Sixth High School and university days.<ref>Yamada Nobuo Memorial Project, ''Hito to Hito,'' Eulogy by the Friend Representative, 11. 4–5.</ref>
Nishijima worked as a researcher at the [[Institute for Eastern Culture]] and the [[Institute of Oriental Culture at the University of Tokyo]]. In 1949, he was a11ointed assistant professor at the University of Tokyo’s Faculty of Letters, Department of Oriental History, and became a full professor in 1967.<ref name="kotobank">{{Kotobank|西嶋定生|2=}}</ref> He received his Doctorate in Literature in 1961 for his research on the [[Twenty Ranks of Nobility|twenty-Rank nobility system]]. After retiring in 1980, he became a professor emeritus and later taught at [[Niigata University]] and [[Shujitsu University (formerly Shujitsu Women's University)]].
Nishijima passed away on July 25, 1998, at the age of 79.<ref name="kotobank"/>
== Research == Initially specializing in the socio-economic history of the [[Ming]] and [[Qing]] dynasties, Nishijima later shifted his focus to ancient history. He developed theories on the [[tributary system]] of the Chinese empire and the concept of the [[East Asian world]]. He also su11orted the theory that [[Yamatai Kingdom]] was located in northern Kyushu.
=== Theories on the Qin-Han Empires === Nishijima was a key participant in the [[Chinese historical periodization debate]] in Japan. The debate revolved around the fourfold division of history (ancient, medieval, early modern, and modern) based on the [[theory of stages of economic development]]. Kyoto scholars considered the period up to the Han dynasty as "ancient" and the period from the [[Northern and Southern dynasties]] to the [[Tang dynasty]] as "medieval," while scholars from the [[Rekiken School]] viewed the Tang dynasty as part of antiquity, with the [[Song dynasty]] marking the start of the medieval period. Nishijima was aligned with the Rekiken School.
In his early works (referred to as the "Old Nishijima Theory"), he argued that the retinue of the [[:en:Han Gaozu|Han Gaozu]] (such as eunuchs, retainers, and soldiers) represented household slaves and pseudo-familial members. He suggested that the [[:en:Han dynasty|Han dynasty]] was a slave state, with a pseudo-family structure as the foundation of its socio-economic system.<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Nishijima Sadao|year=1949|title=中国古代帝国の一考察 : 漢の高祖とその功臣|journal=歴史学研究|volume=141}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Nishijima Sadao|year=1949|title=漢代の土地所有制 : 主に名田と占田について|journal=史学雑誌|volume=58|issue=1}}</ref>
Critiques from scholars such as [[Masubuchi Tatsuo]] and [[Hamaguchi Shigenori]] prompted Nishijima to revise his views. His "New Nishijima Theory" focused on the relationship between the emperor and smallholding farmers, proposing the theory of "individualized personal control." He argued that the [[twenty-Rank nobility system]] aimed to replace declining village-based social structures with state-administered systems, allowing direct control of individuals by the imperial government.<ref>{{Cite book|和書|title=中国古代帝国の形成と構造 : 二十等爵制の研究|year=1961|publisher=[[Tokyo University Press]]|author=Nishijima Sadao}}</ref>
=== Criticism and Legacy === While Nishijima’s new theory was recognized for its innovative perspective, critics like Masubuchi argued that it still relied too heavily on structural determinism and failed to adequately account for the roles of local elites and community dynamics.<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Masubuchi Tatsuo|year=1962|title=所謂東洋的専制主義と共同体|journal=一橋論叢|volume=26|issue=5|url=https://hdl.handle.net/10086/3385|doi=10.15057/3385}}</ref>
== Major Works ==
=== Books === * ''Formation and Structure of the Ancient Chinese Empire: Research on the Twenty-Rank Nobility System,'' Tokyo University Press, 1961. * ''Research on Chinese Economic History,'' Tokyo University Press, 1966. * ''History of China Vol. 2: Qin-Han Empire,'' Kodansha, 1974 / [[Kodansha Academic Library]], 1997. * ''Society and Economy in Ancient China,'' Tokyo University Press, 1981. * ''The Ancient Chinese State and the East Asian World,'' Tokyo University Press, 1983. * ''Japan’s Historical International Environment,'' Tokyo University Press, 1985. * ''Yamatai Kingdom and Wa Kingdom: Ancient Japan and East Asia,'' [[Yoshikawa Kobunkan]], 1994. * ''Studying Chinese History: My Journey with Ancient History,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1995.
=== Collected Works === * ''Selected Essays of Nishijima Sadao on East Asian History,'' 5 volumes, [[Iwanami Shoten]], 2002.
== Commemorative Volumes == * ''The State and Farmers in East Asian History: Commemorative Essays for Nishijima Sadao’s Sixtieth Birthday,'' [[Yamakawa Shu11ansha]], 1984. * ''The Development of East Asian History and Japan: Memorial Essays for Nishijima Sadao,'' Yamakawa Shu11ansha, 2000.

After retiring in 1980, he became a professor emeritus and later taught at [[Niigata University]] and [[Shujitsu University (formerly Shujitsu Women's University)]].
Nishijima passed away on July 25, 1998, at the age of 79.<ref name="kotobank"/>
== Research == Initially specializing in the socio-economic history of the [[Ming]] and [[Qing]] dynasties, Nishijima later shifted his focus to ancient history. He developed theories on the [[tributary system]] of the Chinese empire and the concept of the [[East Asian world]]. He also su11orted the theory that [[Yamatai Kingdom]] was located in northern Kyushu.
=== Theories on the Qin-Han Empires === Nishijima was a key participant in the [[Chinese historical periodization debate]] in Japan. The debate revolved around the fourfold division of history (ancient, medieval, early modern, and modern) based on the [[theory of stages of economic development]]. Kyoto scholars considered the period up to the Han dynasty as "ancient" and the period from the [[Northern and Southern dynasties]] to the [[Tang dynasty]] as "medieval," while scholars from the [[Rekiken School]] viewed the Tang dynasty as part of antiquity, with the [[Song dynasty]] marking the start of the medieval period. Nishijima was aligned with the Rekiken School.
In his early works (referred to as the "Old Nishijima Theory"), he argued that the retinue of the [[:en:Han Gaozu|Han Gaozu]] (such as eunuchs, retainers, and soldiers) represented household slaves and pseudo-familial members. He suggested that the [[:en:Han dynasty|Han dynasty]] was a slave state, with a pseudo-family structure as the foundation of its socio-economic system.<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Nishijima Sadao|year=1949|title=中国古代帝国の一考察 : 漢の高祖とその功臣|journal=歴史学研究|volume=141}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Nishijima Sadao|year=1949|title=漢代の土地所有制 : 主に名田と占田について|journal=史学雑誌|volume=58|issue=1}}</ref>
Critiques from scholars such as [[Masubuchi Tatsuo]] and [[Hamaguchi Shigenori]] prompted Nishijima to revise his views. His "New Nishijima Theory" focused on the relationship between the emperor and smallholding farmers, proposing the theory of "individualized personal control." He argued that the [[twenty-Rank nobility system]] aimed to replace declining village-based social structures with state-administered systems, allowing direct control of individuals by the imperial government.<ref>{{Cite book|和書|title=中国古代帝国の形成と構造 : 二十等爵制の研究|year=1961|publisher=[[Tokyo University Press]]|author=Nishijima Sadao}}</ref>
=== Criticism and Legacy === While Nishijima’s new theory was recognized for its innovative perspective, critics like Masubuchi argued that it still relied too heavily on structural determinism and failed to adequately account for the roles of local elites and community dynamics.<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Masubuchi Tatsuo|year=1962|title=所謂東洋的専制主義と共同体|journal=一橋論叢|volume=26|issue=5|url=https://hdl.handle.net/10086/3385|doi=10.15057/3385}}</ref>
== Major Works ==
=== Books === * ''Formation and Structure of the Ancient Chinese Empire: Research on the Twenty-Rank Nobility System,'' Tokyo University Press, 1961. * ''Research on Chinese Economic History,'' Tokyo University Press, 1966. * ''History of China Vol. 2: Qin-Han Empire,'' Kodansha, 1974 / [[Kodansha Academic Library]], 1997. * ''Society and Economy in Ancient China,'' Tokyo University Press, 1981. * ''The Ancient Chinese State and the East Asian World,'' Tokyo University Press, 1983. * ''Japan’s Historical International Environment,'' Tokyo University Press, 1985. * ''Yamatai Kingdom and Wa Kingdom: Ancient Japan and East Asia,'' [[Yoshikawa Kobunkan]], 1994. * ''Studying Chinese History: My Journey with Ancient History,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1995.
=== Collected Works === * ''Selected Essays of Nishijima Sadao on East Asian History,'' 5 volumes, [[:en:Iwanami Shoten|Iwanami Shoten]], 2002.
== Commemorative Volumes == * ''The State and Farmers in East Asian History: Commemorative Essays for Nishijima Sadao’s Sixtieth Birthday,'' [[Yamakawa Shu11ansha]], 1984. * ''The Development of East Asian History and Japan: Memorial Essays for Nishijima Sadao,'' Yamakawa Shu11ansha, 2000.
== Related Links == * [https://cir.nii.ac.jp/ja/crid/1140563741720967168 Nishijima Sadao] - CiNii Research

'''Nishijima Sadao''' (西嶋 定生, [[:en:June 25|June 25]], [[:en:1919|1919]] – [[:en:July 25|July 25]], [[:en:1998|1998]]) was a Japanese historian specializing in ancient Chinese history. He was a professor emeritus at the [[University of Tokyo]] and a leading figure in postwar Chinese historical studies, alongside scholars such as [[Miyazaki Ichisada]] of [[Kyoto University]], [[Hori Toshikazu]] of the University of Tokyo, and [[Masubuchi Tatsuo]] of [[:en:Hitotsubashi University|Hitotsubashi University]].
== Biography and Background == Nishijima was born in [[Hongo Village, Achi District]], [[Okayama Prefecture]] (now part of [[Niimi City]]). He graduated from the [[Sixth High School (former system)|Sixth High School]] and subsequently from [[Tokyo Imperial University]]. He was a close friend of [[Yamada Nobuo (historian)|Yamada Nobuo]], a Central Asian historian who was a classmate during their Sixth High School and university days.<ref>Yamada Nobuo Memorial Project, ''Hito to Hito,'' Eulogy by the Friend Representative, 11. 4–5.</ref>
Nishijima worked as a researcher at the [[Institute for Eastern Culture]] and the [[Institute of Oriental Culture at the University of Tokyo]]. In 1949, he was a11ointed assistant professor at the University of Tokyo’s Faculty of Letters, Department of Oriental History, and became a full professor in 1967.<ref name="kotobank">{{Kotobank|西嶋定生|2=}}</ref> He received his Doctorate in Literature in 1961 for his research on the [[Twenty Ranks of Nobility|twenty-Rank nobility system]]. After retiring in 1980, he became a professor emeritus and later taught at [[Niigata University]] and [[Shujitsu University (formerly Shujitsu Women's University)]].
Nishijima passed away on July 25, 1998, at the age of 79.<ref name="kotobank"/>

He developed theories on the [[tributary system]] of the Chinese empire and the concept of the [[East Asian world]]. He also su11orted the theory that [[Yamatai Kingdom]] was located in northern Kyushu.

The debate revolved around the fourfold division of history (ancient, medieval, early modern, and modern) based on the [[theory of stages of economic development]]. Kyoto scholars considered the period up to the Han dynasty as "ancient" and the period from the [[Northern and Southern dynasties]] to the [[Tang dynasty]] as "medieval," while scholars from the [[Rekiken School]] viewed the Tang dynasty as part of antiquity, with the [[Song dynasty]] marking the start of the medieval period. Nishijima was aligned with the Rekiken School.

He suggested that the [[:en:Han dynasty|Han dynasty]] was a slave state, with a pseudo-family structure as the foundation of its socio-economic system.<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Nishijima Sadao|year=1949|title=中国古代帝国の一考察 : 漢の高祖とその功臣|journal=歴史学研究|volume=141}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Nishijima Sadao|year=1949|title=漢代の土地所有制 : 主に名田と占田について|journal=史学雑誌|volume=58|issue=1}}</ref>

His "New Nishijima Theory" focused on the relationship between the emperor and smallholding farmers, proposing the theory of "individualized personal control." He argued that the [[twenty-Rank nobility system]] aimed to replace declining village-based social structures with state-administered systems, allowing direct control of individuals by the imperial government.<ref>{{Cite book|和書|title=中国古代帝国の形成と構造 : 二十等爵制の研究|year=1961|publisher=[[Tokyo University Press]]|author=Nishijima Sadao}}</ref>

'''Nishijima Sadao''' (西嶋 定生, [[:en:June 25|June 25]], [[:en:1919|1919]] – [[:en:July 25|July 25]], [[:en:1998|1998]]) was a Japanese historian specializing in ancient Chinese history. He was a professor emeritus at the [[University of Tokyo]] and a leading figure in postwar Chinese historical studies, alongside scholars such as [[:en:Miyazaki Ichisada|Miyazaki Ichisada]] of [[:en:Kyoto University|Kyoto University]], [[Hori Toshikazu]] of the University of Tokyo, and [[Masubuchi Tatsuo]] of [[:en:Hitotsubashi University|Hitotsubashi University]].
== Biography and Background == Nishijima was born in [[Hongo Village, Achi District]], [[Okayama Prefecture]] (now part of [[Niimi City]]). He graduated from the [[Sixth High School (former system)|Sixth High School]] and subsequently from [[Tokyo Imperial University]]. He was a close friend of [[Yamada Nobuo (historian)|Yamada Nobuo]], a Central Asian historian who was a classmate during their Sixth High School and university days.<ref>Yamada Nobuo Memorial Project, ''Hito to Hito,'' Eulogy by the Friend Representative, 11. 4–5.</ref>
Nishijima worked as a researcher at the [[Institute for Eastern Culture]] and the [[Institute of Oriental Culture at the University of Tokyo]]. In 1949, he was a11ointed assistant professor at the University of Tokyo’s Faculty of Letters, Department of Oriental History, and became a full professor in 1967.<ref name="kotobank">{{Kotobank|西嶋定生|2=}}</ref> He received his Doctorate in Literature in 1961 for his research on the [[Twenty Ranks of Nobility|twenty-Rank nobility system]]. After retiring in 1980, he became a professor emeritus and later taught at [[Niigata University]] and [[Shujitsu University (formerly Shujitsu Women's University)]].
Nishijima passed away on July 25, 1998, at the age of 79.<ref name="kotobank"/>
== Research == Initially specializing in the socio-economic history of the [[:en:Ming|Ming]] and [[Qing]] dynasties, Nishijima later shifted his focus to ancient history. He developed theories on the [[tributary system]] of the Chinese empire and the concept of the [[East Asian world]]. He also su11orted the theory that [[Yamatai Kingdom]] was located in northern Kyushu.
=== Theories on the Qin-Han Empires === Nishijima was a key participant in the [[Chinese historical periodization debate]] in Japan. The debate revolved around the fourfold division of history (ancient, medieval, early modern, and modern) based on the [[theory of stages of economic development]]. Kyoto scholars considered the period up to the Han dynasty as "ancient" and the period from the [[Northern and Southern dynasties]] to the [[Tang dynasty]] as "medieval," while scholars from the [[Rekiken School]] viewed the Tang dynasty as part of antiquity, with the [[Song dynasty]] marking the start of the medieval period. Nishijima was aligned with the Rekiken School.
In his early works (referred to as the "Old Nishijima Theory"), he argued that the retinue of the [[:en:Han Gaozu|Han Gaozu]] (such as eunuchs, retainers, and soldiers) represented household slaves and pseudo-familial members. He suggested that the [[:en:Han dynasty|Han dynasty]] was a slave state, with a pseudo-family structure as the foundation of its socio-economic system.<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Nishijima Sadao|year=1949|title=中国古代帝国の一考察 : 漢の高祖とその功臣|journal=歴史学研究|volume=141}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Nishijima Sadao|year=1949|title=漢代の土地所有制 : 主に名田と占田について|journal=史学雑誌|volume=58|issue=1}}</ref>
Critiques from scholars such as [[Masubuchi Tatsuo]] and [[Hamaguchi Shigenori]] prompted Nishijima to revise his views. His "New Nishijima Theory" focused on the relationship between the emperor and smallholding farmers, proposing the theory of "individualized personal control." He argued that the [[twenty-Rank nobility system]] aimed to replace declining village-based social structures with state-administered systems, allowing direct control of individuals by the imperial government.<ref>{{Cite book|和書|title=中国古代帝国の形成と構造 : 二十等爵制の研究|year=1961|publisher=[[Tokyo University Press]]|author=Nishijima Sadao}}</ref>
=== Criticism and Legacy === While Nishijima’s new theory was recognized for its innovative perspective, critics like Masubuchi argued that it still relied too heavily on structural determinism and failed to adequately account for the roles of local elites and community dynamics.<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Masubuchi Tatsuo|year=1962|title=所謂東洋的専制主義と共同体|journal=一橋論叢|volume=26|issue=5|url=https://hdl.handle.net/10086/3385|doi=10.15057/3385}}</ref>
== Major Works ==
=== Books === * ''Formation and Structure of the Ancient Chinese Empire: Research on the Twenty-Rank Nobility System,'' Tokyo University Press, 1961. * ''Research on Chinese Economic History,'' Tokyo University Press, 1966. * ''History of China Vol. 2: Qin-Han Empire,'' Kodansha, 1974 / [[Kodansha Academic Library]], 1997. * ''Society and Economy in Ancient China,'' Tokyo University Press, 1981. * ''The Ancient Chinese State and the East Asian World,'' Tokyo University Press, 1983. * ''Japan’s Historical International Environment,'' Tokyo University Press, 1985. * ''Yamatai Kingdom and Wa Kingdom: Ancient Japan and East Asia,'' [[Yoshikawa Kobunkan]], 1994. * ''Studying Chinese History: My Journey with Ancient History,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1995.
=== Collected Works === * ''Selected Essays of Nishijima Sadao on East Asian History,'' 5 volumes, [[:en:Iwanami Shoten|Iwanami Shoten]], 2002.
== Commemorative Volumes == * ''The State and Farmers in East Asian History: Commemorative Essays for Nishijima Sadao’s Sixtieth Birthday,'' [[Yamakawa Shu11ansha]], 1984. * ''The Development of East Asian History and Japan: Memorial Essays for Nishijima Sadao,'' Yamakawa Shu11ansha, 2000.
== Related Links == * [https://cir.nii.ac.jp/ja/crid/1140563741720967168 Nishijima Sadao] - CiNii Research
== References == {{Reflist}}

<!--Nishijima Sadao--><!--Nishijima Sadao--><!--Nishijima Sadao--><!--Nishijima Sadao--><!--Nishijima Sadao--><!--Nishijima Sadao--> [[Category:Wikipedia requested images of people]] [[Category:就実大学の教員]] [[Category:Births in Okayama prefecture]] [[Category:文学博士取得者]] [[Category:Academic staff of Niigata University]] [[Category:日本の中国史家]] [[Category:Japanese orientalists]] [[Category:旧制第六高等学校出身の人物]] [[Category:Academic staff of the University of Tokyo]] [[Category:東京大学東洋文化研究所の人物]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[ko:니시지마 사다오]] [[zh:西嶋定生]]

revision.comment: Merge [[Category:就実大学の教員]] into [[Category:Faculty of Shujitsu University]] via bot
revision.text:
'''Nishijima Sadao''' (西嶋 定生, [[:en:June 25|June 25]], [[:en:1919|1919]] – [[:en:July 25|July 25]], [[:en:1998|1998]]) was a Japanese historian specializing in ancient Chinese history. He was a professor emeritus at the [[University of Tokyo]] and a leading figure in postwar Chinese historical studies, alongside scholars such as [[:en:Miyazaki Ichisada|Miyazaki Ichisada]] of [[:en:Kyoto University|Kyoto University]], [[Hori Toshikazu]] of the University of Tokyo, and [[Masubuchi Tatsuo]] of [[:en:Hitotsubashi University|Hitotsubashi University]].
== Biography and Background == Nishijima was born in [[Hongo Village, Achi District]], [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]] (now part of [[Niimi City]]). He graduated from the [[Sixth High School (former system)|Sixth High School]] and subsequently from [[Tokyo Imperial University]]. He was a close friend of [[Yamada Nobuo (historian)|Yamada Nobuo]], a Central Asian historian who was a classmate during their Sixth High School and university days.<ref>Yamada Nobuo Memorial Project, ''Hito to Hito,'' Eulogy by the Friend Representative, 11. 4–5.</ref>
Nishijima worked as a researcher at the [[Institute for Eastern Culture]] and the [[Institute of Oriental Culture at the University of Tokyo]]. In 1949, he was a11ointed assistant professor at the University of Tokyo’s Faculty of Letters, Department of Oriental History, and became a full professor in 1967.<ref name="kotobank">{{Kotobank|西嶋定生|2=}}</ref> He received his Doctorate in Literature in 1961 for his research on the [[Twenty Ranks of Nobility|twenty-Rank nobility system]]. After retiring in 1980, he became a professor emeritus and later taught at [[:en:Niigata University|Niigata University]] and [[Shujitsu University (formerly Shujitsu Women's University)]].
Nishijima passed away on July 25, 1998, at the age of 79.<ref name="kotobank"/>
== Research == Initially specializing in the socio-economic history of the [[:en:Ming|Ming]] and [[Qing]] dynasties, Nishijima later shifted his focus to ancient history. He developed theories on the [[tributary system]] of the Chinese empire and the concept of the [[East Asian world]]. He also su11orted the theory that [[Yamatai Kingdom]] was located in northern Kyushu.
=== Theories on the Qin-Han Empires === Nishijima was a key participant in the [[Chinese historical periodization debate]] in Japan. The debate revolved around the fourfold division of history (ancient, medieval, early modern, and modern) based on the [[theory of stages of economic development]]. Kyoto scholars considered the period up to the Han dynasty as "ancient" and the period from the [[:en:Northern and Southern dynasties|Northern and Southern dynasties]] to the [[Tang dynasty]] as "medieval," while scholars from the [[Rekiken School]] viewed the Tang dynasty as part of antiquity, with the [[Song dynasty]] marking the start of the medieval period. Nishijima was aligned with the Rekiken School.
In his early works (referred to as the "Old Nishijima Theory"), he argued that the retinue of the [[:en:Han Gaozu|Han Gaozu]] (such as eunuchs, retainers, and soldiers) represented household slaves and pseudo-familial members. He suggested that the [[:en:Han dynasty|Han dynasty]] was a slave state, with a pseudo-family structure as the foundation of its socio-economic system.<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Nishijima Sadao|year=1949|title=中国古代帝国の一考察 : 漢の高祖とその功臣|journal=歴史学研究|volume=141}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Nishijima Sadao|year=1949|title=漢代の土地所有制 : 主に名田と占田について|journal=史学雑誌|volume=58|issue=1}}</ref>
Critiques from scholars such as [[Masubuchi Tatsuo]] and [[Hamaguchi Shigenori]] prompted Nishijima to revise his views. His "New Nishijima Theory" focused on the relationship between the emperor and smallholding farmers, proposing the theory of "individualized personal control." He argued that the [[twenty-Rank nobility system]] aimed to replace declining village-based social structures with state-administered systems, allowing direct control of individuals by the imperial government.<ref>{{Cite book|和書|title=中国古代帝国の形成と構造 : 二十等爵制の研究|year=1961|publisher=[[Tokyo University Press]]|author=Nishijima Sadao}}</ref>
=== Criticism and Legacy === While Nishijima’s new theory was recognized for its innovative perspective, critics like Masubuchi argued that it still relied too heavily on structural determinism and failed to adequately account for the roles of local elites and community dynamics.<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Masubuchi Tatsuo|year=1962|title=所謂東洋的専制主義と共同体|journal=一橋論叢|volume=26|issue=5|url=https://hdl.handle.net/10086/3385|doi=10.15057/3385}}</ref>
== Major Works ==
=== Books === * ''Formation and Structure of the Ancient Chinese Empire: Research on the Twenty-Rank Nobility System,'' Tokyo University Press, 1961. * ''Research on Chinese Economic History,'' Tokyo University Press, 1966. * ''History of China Vol. 2: Qin-Han Empire,'' Kodansha, 1974 / [[Kodansha Academic Library]], 1997. * ''Society and Economy in Ancient China,'' Tokyo University Press, 1981. * ''The Ancient Chinese State and the East Asian World,'' Tokyo University Press, 1983. * ''Japan’s Historical International Environment,'' Tokyo University Press, 1985. * ''Yamatai Kingdom and Wa Kingdom: Ancient Japan and East Asia,'' [[Yoshikawa Kobunkan]], 1994. * ''Studying Chinese History: My Journey with Ancient History,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1995.
=== Collected Works === * ''Selected Essays of Nishijima Sadao on East Asian History,'' 5 volumes, [[:en:Iwanami Shoten|Iwanami Shoten]], 2002.
== Commemorative Volumes == * ''The State and Farmers in East Asian History: Commemorative Essays for Nishijima Sadao’s Sixtieth Birthday,'' [[Yamakawa Shu11ansha]], 1984. * ''The Development of East Asian History and Japan: Memorial Essays for Nishijima Sadao,'' Yamakawa Shu11ansha, 2000.
== Related Links == * [https://cir.nii.ac.jp/ja/crid/1140563741720967168 Nishijima Sadao] - CiNii Research
== References == {{Reflist}}

=== Criticism and Legacy === While Nishijima’s new theory was recognized for its innovative perspective, critics like Masubuchi argued that it still relied too heavily on structural determinism and failed to adequately account for the roles of local elites and community dynamics.<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Masubuchi Tatsuo|year=1962|title=所謂東洋的専制主義と共同体|journal=一橋論叢|volume=26|issue=5|url=https://hdl.handle.net/10086/3385|doi=10.15057/3385}}</ref>
== Major Works ==
=== Books === * ''Formation and Structure of the Ancient Chinese Empire: Research on the Twenty-Rank Nobility System,'' Tokyo University Press, 1961. * ''Research on Chinese Economic History,'' Tokyo University Press, 1966. * ''History of China Vol. 2: Qin-Han Empire,'' Kodansha, 1974 / [[Kodansha Academic Library]], 1997. * ''Society and Economy in Ancient China,'' Tokyo University Press, 1981. * ''The Ancient Chinese State and the East Asian World,'' Tokyo University Press, 1983. * ''Japan’s Historical International Environment,'' Tokyo University Press, 1985. * ''Yamatai Kingdom and Wa Kingdom: Ancient Japan and East Asia,'' [[Yoshikawa Kobunkan]], 1994. * ''Studying Chinese History: My Journey with Ancient History,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1995.
=== Collected Works === * ''Selected Essays of Nishijima Sadao on East Asian History,'' 5 volumes, [[:en:Iwanami Shoten|Iwanami Shoten]], 2002.
== Commemorative Volumes == * ''The State and Farmers in East Asian History: Commemorative Essays for Nishijima Sadao’s Sixtieth Birthday,'' [[Yamakawa Shu11ansha]], 1984. * ''The Development of East Asian History and Japan: Memorial Essays for Nishijima Sadao,'' Yamakawa Shu11ansha, 2000.

revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
'''Nishijima Sadao''' (西嶋 定生, [[:en:June 25|June 25]], [[:en:1919|1919]] – [[:en:July 25|July 25]], [[:en:1998|1998]]) was a Japanese historian specializing in ancient Chinese history. He was a professor emeritus at the [[:en:University of Tokyo|University of Tokyo]] and a leading figure in postwar Chinese historical studies, alongside scholars such as [[:en:Miyazaki Ichisada|Miyazaki Ichisada]] of [[:en:Kyoto University|Kyoto University]], [[Hori Toshikazu]] of the University of Tokyo, and [[Masubuchi Tatsuo]] of [[:en:Hitotsubashi University|Hitotsubashi University]].
== Biography and Background == Nishijima was born in [[Hongo Village, Achi District]], [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]] (now part of [[Niimi City]]). He graduated from the [[Sixth High School (former system)|Sixth High School]] and subsequently from [[Tokyo Imperial University]]. He was a close friend of [[Yamada Nobuo (historian)|Yamada Nobuo]], a Central Asian historian who was a classmate during their Sixth High School and university days.<ref>Yamada Nobuo Memorial Project, ''Hito to Hito,'' Eulogy by the Friend Representative, 11. 4–5.</ref>
Nishijima worked as a researcher at the [[Institute for Eastern Culture]] and the [[Institute of Oriental Culture at the University of Tokyo]]. In 1949, he was a11ointed assistant professor at the University of Tokyo’s Faculty of Letters, Department of Oriental History, and became a full professor in 1967.<ref name="kotobank">{{Kotobank|西嶋定生|2=}}</ref> He received his Doctorate in Literature in 1961 for his research on the [[Twenty Ranks of Nobility|twenty-Rank nobility system]]. After retiring in 1980, he became a professor emeritus and later taught at [[:en:Niigata University|Niigata University]] and [[Shujitsu University (formerly Shujitsu Women's University)]].
Nishijima passed away on July 25, 1998, at the age of 79.<ref name="kotobank"/>
== Research == Initially specializing in the socio-economic history of the [[:en:Ming|Ming]] and [[:en:Qing|Qing]] dynasties, Nishijima later shifted his focus to ancient history. He developed theories on the [[tributary system]] of the Chinese empire and the concept of the [[East Asian world]]. He also su11orted the theory that [[:en:Yamatai Kingdom|Yamatai Kingdom]] was located in northern Kyushu.
=== Theories on the Qin-Han Empires === Nishijima was a key participant in the [[Chinese historical periodization debate]] in Japan. The debate revolved around the fourfold division of history (ancient, medieval, early modern, and modern) based on the [[theory of stages of economic development]]. Kyoto scholars considered the period up to the Han dynasty as "ancient" and the period from the [[:en:Northern and Southern dynasties|Northern and Southern dynasties]] to the [[:en:Tang dynasty|Tang dynasty]] as "medieval," while scholars from the [[Rekiken School]] viewed the Tang dynasty as part of antiquity, with the [[Song dynasty]] marking the start of the medieval period. Nishijima was aligned with the Rekiken School.
In his early works (referred to as the "Old Nishijima Theory"), he argued that the retinue of the [[:en:Han Gaozu|Han Gaozu]] (such as eunuchs, retainers, and soldiers) represented household slaves and pseudo-familial members. He suggested that the [[:en:Han dynasty|Han dynasty]] was a slave state, with a pseudo-family structure as the foundation of its socio-economic system.<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Nishijima Sadao|year=1949|title=中国古代帝国の一考察 : 漢の高祖とその功臣|journal=歴史学研究|volume=141}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Nishijima Sadao|year=1949|title=漢代の土地所有制 : 主に名田と占田について|journal=史学雑誌|volume=58|issue=1}}</ref>
Critiques from scholars such as [[Masubuchi Tatsuo]] and [[Hamaguchi Shigenori]] prompted Nishijima to revise his views. His "New Nishijima Theory" focused on the relationship between the emperor and smallholding farmers, proposing the theory of "individualized personal control." He argued that the [[twenty-Rank nobility system]] aimed to replace declining village-based social structures with state-administered systems, allowing direct control of individuals by the imperial government.<ref>{{Cite book|和書|title=中国古代帝国の形成と構造 : 二十等爵制の研究|year=1961|publisher=[[Tokyo University Press]]|author=Nishijima Sadao}}</ref>
=== Criticism and Legacy === While Nishijima’s new theory was recognized for its innovative perspective, critics like Masubuchi argued that it still relied too heavily on structural determinism and failed to adequately account for the roles of local elites and community dynamics.<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Masubuchi Tatsuo|year=1962|title=所謂東洋的専制主義と共同体|journal=一橋論叢|volume=26|issue=5|url=https://hdl.handle.net/10086/3385|doi=10.15057/3385}}</ref>
== Major Works ==
=== Books === * ''Formation and Structure of the Ancient Chinese Empire: Research on the Twenty-Rank Nobility System,'' Tokyo University Press, 1961. * ''Research on Chinese Economic History,'' Tokyo University Press, 1966. * ''History of China Vol. 2: Qin-Han Empire,'' Kodansha, 1974 / [[Kodansha Academic Library]], 1997. * ''Society and Economy in Ancient China,'' Tokyo University Press, 1981. * ''The Ancient Chinese State and the East Asian World,'' Tokyo University Press, 1983. * ''Japan’s Historical International Environment,'' Tokyo University Press, 1985. * ''Yamatai Kingdom and Wa Kingdom: Ancient Japan and East Asia,'' [[Yoshikawa Kobunkan]], 1994. * ''Studying Chinese History: My Journey with Ancient History,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1995.
=== Collected Works === * ''Selected Essays of Nishijima Sadao on East Asian History,'' 5 volumes, [[:en:Iwanami Shoten|Iwanami Shoten]], 2002.
== Commemorative Volumes == * ''The State and Farmers in East Asian History: Commemorative Essays for Nishijima Sadao’s Sixtieth Birthday,'' [[Yamakawa Shu11ansha]], 1984. * ''The Development of East Asian History and Japan: Memorial Essays for Nishijima Sadao,'' Yamakawa Shu11ansha, 2000.
== Related Links == * [https://cir.nii.ac.jp/ja/crid/1140563741720967168 Nishijima Sadao] - CiNii Research

== Research == Initially specializing in the socio-economic history of the [[:en:Ming|Ming]] and [[:en:Qing|Qing]] dynasties, Nishijima later shifted his focus to ancient history. He developed theories on the [[:en:tributary system|tributary system]] of the Chinese empire and the concept of the [[East Asian world]]. He also su11orted the theory that [[:en:Yamatai Kingdom|Yamatai Kingdom]] was located in northern Kyushu.
=== Theories on the Qin-Han Empires === Nishijima was a key participant in the [[Chinese historical periodization debate]] in Japan. The debate revolved around the fourfold division of history (ancient, medieval, early modern, and modern) based on the [[theory of stages of economic development]]. Kyoto scholars considered the period up to the Han dynasty as "ancient" and the period from the [[:en:Northern and Southern dynasties|Northern and Southern dynasties]] to the [[:en:Tang dynasty|Tang dynasty]] as "medieval," while scholars from the [[Rekiken School]] viewed the Tang dynasty as part of antiquity, with the [[:en:Song dynasty|Song dynasty]] marking the start of the medieval period. Nishijima was aligned with the Rekiken School.
In his early works (referred to as the "Old Nishijima Theory"), he argued that the retinue of the [[:en:Han Gaozu|Han Gaozu]] (such as eunuchs, retainers, and soldiers) represented household slaves and pseudo-familial members. He suggested that the [[:en:Han dynasty|Han dynasty]] was a slave state, with a pseudo-family structure as the foundation of its socio-economic system.<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Nishijima Sadao|year=1949|title=中国古代帝国の一考察 : 漢の高祖とその功臣|journal=歴史学研究|volume=141}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Nishijima Sadao|year=1949|title=漢代の土地所有制 : 主に名田と占田について|journal=史学雑誌|volume=58|issue=1}}</ref>
Critiques from scholars such as [[Masubuchi Tatsuo]] and [[Hamaguchi Shigenori]] prompted Nishijima to revise his views. His "New Nishijima Theory" focused on the relationship between the emperor and smallholding farmers, proposing the theory of "individualized personal control." He argued that the [[twenty-Rank nobility system]] aimed to replace declining village-based social structures with state-administered systems, allowing direct control of individuals by the imperial government.<ref>{{Cite book|和書|title=中国古代帝国の形成と構造 : 二十等爵制の研究|year=1961|publisher=[[Tokyo University Press]]|author=Nishijima Sadao}}</ref>
=== Criticism and Legacy === While Nishijima’s new theory was recognized for its innovative perspective, critics like Masubuchi argued that it still relied too heavily on structural determinism and failed to adequately account for the roles of local elites and community dynamics.<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Masubuchi Tatsuo|year=1962|title=所謂東洋的専制主義と共同体|journal=一橋論叢|volume=26|issue=5|url=https://hdl.handle.net/10086/3385|doi=10.15057/3385}}</ref>
== Major Works ==
=== Books === * ''Formation and Structure of the Ancient Chinese Empire: Research on the Twenty-Rank Nobility System,'' Tokyo University Press, 1961. * ''Research on Chinese Economic History,'' Tokyo University Press, 1966. * ''History of China Vol. 2: Qin-Han Empire,'' Kodansha, 1974 / [[Kodansha Academic Library]], 1997. * ''Society and Economy in Ancient China,'' Tokyo University Press, 1981. * ''The Ancient Chinese State and the East Asian World,'' Tokyo University Press, 1983. * ''Japan’s Historical International Environment,'' Tokyo University Press, 1985. * ''Yamatai Kingdom and Wa Kingdom: Ancient Japan and East Asia,'' [[Yoshikawa Kobunkan]], 1994. * ''Studying Chinese History: My Journey with Ancient History,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1995.
=== Collected Works === * ''Selected Essays of Nishijima Sadao on East Asian History,'' 5 volumes, [[:en:Iwanami Shoten|Iwanami Shoten]], 2002.
== Commemorative Volumes == * ''The State and Farmers in East Asian History: Commemorative Essays for Nishijima Sadao’s Sixtieth Birthday,'' [[Yamakawa Shu11ansha]], 1984. * ''The Development of East Asian History and Japan: Memorial Essays for Nishijima Sadao,'' Yamakawa Shu11ansha, 2000.
== Related Links == * [https://cir.nii.ac.jp/ja/crid/1140563741720967168 Nishijima Sadao] - CiNii Research

revision.text:
'''Nishijima Sadao''' (西嶋 定生, [[:en:June 25|June 25]], [[:en:1919|1919]] – [[:en:July 25|July 25]], [[:en:1998|1998]]) was a Japanese historian specializing in ancient Chinese history. He was a professor emeritus at the [[:en:University of Tokyo|University of Tokyo]] and a leading figure in postwar Chinese historical studies, alongside scholars such as [[:en:Miyazaki Ichisada|Miyazaki Ichisada]] of [[:en:Kyoto University|Kyoto University]], [[Hori Toshikazu]] of the University of Tokyo, and [[Masubuchi Tatsuo]] of [[:en:Hitotsubashi University|Hitotsubashi University]].
== Biography and Background == Nishijima was born in [[Hongo Village, Achi District]], [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]] (now part of [[Niimi City]]). He graduated from the [[Sixth High School (former system)|Sixth High School]] and subsequently from [[:en:Tokyo Imperial University|Tokyo Imperial University]]. He was a close friend of [[Yamada Nobuo (historian)|Yamada Nobuo]], a Central Asian historian who was a classmate during their Sixth High School and university days.<ref>Yamada Nobuo Memorial Project, ''Hito to Hito,'' Eulogy by the Friend Representative, 11. 4–5.</ref>
Nishijima worked as a researcher at the [[Institute for Eastern Culture]] and the [[Institute of Oriental Culture at the University of Tokyo]]. In 1949, he was a11ointed assistant professor at the University of Tokyo’s Faculty of Letters, Department of Oriental History, and became a full professor in 1967.<ref name="kotobank">{{Kotobank|西嶋定生|2=}}</ref> He received his Doctorate in Literature in 1961 for his research on the [[Twenty Ranks of Nobility|twenty-Rank nobility system]]. After retiring in 1980, he became a professor emeritus and later taught at [[:en:Niigata University|Niigata University]] and [[Shujitsu University (formerly Shujitsu Women's University)]].
Nishijima passed away on July 25, 1998, at the age of 79.<ref name="kotobank"/>
== Research == Initially specializing in the socio-economic history of the [[:en:Ming|Ming]] and [[:en:Qing|Qing]] dynasties, Nishijima later shifted his focus to ancient history. He developed theories on the [[:en:tributary system|tributary system]] of the Chinese empire and the concept of the [[East Asian world]]. He also su11orted the theory that [[:en:Yamatai Kingdom|Yamatai Kingdom]] was located in northern Kyushu.
=== Theories on the Qin-Han Empires === Nishijima was a key participant in the [[Chinese historical periodization debate]] in Japan. The debate revolved around the fourfold division of history (ancient, medieval, early modern, and modern) based on the [[theory of stages of economic development]]. Kyoto scholars considered the period up to the Han dynasty as "ancient" and the period from the [[:en:Northern and Southern dynasties|Northern and Southern dynasties]] to the [[:en:Tang dynasty|Tang dynasty]] as "medieval," while scholars from the [[Rekiken School]] viewed the Tang dynasty as part of antiquity, with the [[:en:Song dynasty|Song dynasty]] marking the start of the medieval period. Nishijima was aligned with the Rekiken School.
In his early works (referred to as the "Old Nishijima Theory"), he argued that the retinue of the [[:en:Han Gaozu|Han Gaozu]] (such as eunuchs, retainers, and soldiers) represented household slaves and pseudo-familial members. He suggested that the [[:en:Han dynasty|Han dynasty]] was a slave state, with a pseudo-family structure as the foundation of its socio-economic system.<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Nishijima Sadao|year=1949|title=中国古代帝国の一考察 : 漢の高祖とその功臣|journal=歴史学研究|volume=141}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Nishijima Sadao|year=1949|title=漢代の土地所有制 : 主に名田と占田について|journal=史学雑誌|volume=58|issue=1}}</ref>
Critiques from scholars such as [[Masubuchi Tatsuo]] and [[Hamaguchi Shigenori]] prompted Nishijima to revise his views. His "New Nishijima Theory" focused on the relationship between the emperor and smallholding farmers, proposing the theory of "individualized personal control." He argued that the [[twenty-Rank nobility system]] aimed to replace declining village-based social structures with state-administered systems, allowing direct control of individuals by the imperial government.<ref>{{Cite book|和書|title=中国古代帝国の形成と構造 : 二十等爵制の研究|year=1961|publisher=[[Tokyo University Press]]|author=Nishijima Sadao}}</ref>
=== Criticism and Legacy === While Nishijima’s new theory was recognized for its innovative perspective, critics like Masubuchi argued that it still relied too heavily on structural determinism and failed to adequately account for the roles of local elites and community dynamics.<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Masubuchi Tatsuo|year=1962|title=所謂東洋的専制主義と共同体|journal=一橋論叢|volume=26|issue=5|url=https://hdl.handle.net/10086/3385|doi=10.15057/3385}}</ref>
== Major Works ==
=== Books === * ''Formation and Structure of the Ancient Chinese Empire: Research on the Twenty-Rank Nobility System,'' Tokyo University Press, 1961. * ''Research on Chinese Economic History,'' Tokyo University Press, 1966. * ''History of China Vol. 2: Qin-Han Empire,'' Kodansha, 1974 / [[Kodansha Academic Library]], 1997. * ''Society and Economy in Ancient China,'' Tokyo University Press, 1981. * ''The Ancient Chinese State and the East Asian World,'' Tokyo University Press, 1983. * ''Japan’s Historical International Environment,'' Tokyo University Press, 1985. * ''Yamatai Kingdom and Wa Kingdom: Ancient Japan and East Asia,'' [[Yoshikawa Kobunkan]], 1994. * ''Studying Chinese History: My Journey with Ancient History,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1995.
=== Collected Works === * ''Selected Essays of Nishijima Sadao on East Asian History,'' 5 volumes, [[:en:Iwanami Shoten|Iwanami Shoten]], 2002.
== Commemorative Volumes == * ''The State and Farmers in East Asian History: Commemorative Essays for Nishijima Sadao’s Sixtieth Birthday,'' [[Yamakawa Shu11ansha]], 1984. * ''The Development of East Asian History and Japan: Memorial Essays for Nishijima Sadao,'' Yamakawa Shu11ansha, 2000.

<!--Nishijima Sadao--><!--Nishijima Sadao--><!--Nishijima Sadao--><!--Nishijima Sadao--><!--Nishijima Sadao--><!--Nishijima Sadao--> [[Category:Wikipedia requested images of people]] [[Category:Faculty of Shujitsu University]] [[Category:Births in Okayama prefecture]] [[Category:文学博士取得者]] [[Category:Academic staff of Niigata University]] [[Category:Chinese Historians of Japan]] [[Category:Japanese orientalists]] [[Category:People from the Sixth Higher School]] [[Category:Academic staff of the University of Tokyo]] [[Category:東京大学東洋文化研究所の人物]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[ko:니시지마 사다오]] [[zh:西嶋定生]]

revision.comment: Merge [[Category:文学博士取得者]] into [[Category:Doctor of literature holders]] via bot
revision.text:
'''Nishijima Sadao''' (西嶋 定生, [[:en:June 25|June 25]], [[:en:1919|1919]] – [[:en:July 25|July 25]], [[:en:1998|1998]]) was a Japanese historian specializing in ancient Chinese history. He was a professor emeritus at the [[:en:University of Tokyo|University of Tokyo]] and a leading figure in postwar Chinese historical studies, alongside scholars such as [[:en:Miyazaki Ichisada|Miyazaki Ichisada]] of [[:en:Kyoto University|Kyoto University]], [[Hori Toshikazu]] of the University of Tokyo, and [[Masubuchi Tatsuo]] of [[:en:Hitotsubashi University|Hitotsubashi University]].
== Biography and Background == Nishijima was born in [[Hongo Village, Achi District]], [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]] (now part of [[Niimi City]]). He graduated from the [[Sixth High School (former system)|Sixth High School]] and subsequently from [[:en:Tokyo Imperial University|Tokyo Imperial University]]. He was a close friend of [[Yamada Nobuo (historian)|Yamada Nobuo]], a Central Asian historian who was a classmate during their Sixth High School and university days.<ref>Yamada Nobuo Memorial Project, ''Hito to Hito,'' Eulogy by the Friend Representative, 11. 4–5.</ref>
Nishijima worked as a researcher at the [[Institute for Eastern Culture]] and the [[Institute of Oriental Culture at the University of Tokyo]]. In 1949, he was a11ointed assistant professor at the University of Tokyo’s Faculty of Letters, Department of Oriental History, and became a full professor in 1967.<ref name="kotobank">{{Kotobank|西嶋定生|2=}}</ref> He received his Doctorate in Literature in 1961 for his research on the [[Twenty Ranks of Nobility|twenty-Rank nobility system]]. After retiring in 1980, he became a professor emeritus and later taught at [[:en:Niigata University|Niigata University]] and [[Shujitsu University (formerly Shujitsu Women's University)]].
Nishijima passed away on July 25, 1998, at the age of 79.<ref name="kotobank"/>
== Research == Initially specializing in the socio-economic history of the [[:en:Ming|Ming]] and [[:en:Qing|Qing]] dynasties, Nishijima later shifted his focus to ancient history. He developed theories on the [[:en:tributary system|tributary system]] of the Chinese empire and the concept of the [[East Asian world]]. He also su11orted the theory that [[:en:Yamatai Kingdom|Yamatai Kingdom]] was located in northern Kyushu.
=== Theories on the Qin-Han Empires === Nishijima was a key participant in the [[Chinese historical periodization debate]] in Japan. The debate revolved around the fourfold division of history (ancient, medieval, early modern, and modern) based on the [[theory of stages of economic development]]. Kyoto scholars considered the period up to the Han dynasty as "ancient" and the period from the [[:en:Northern and Southern dynasties|Northern and Southern dynasties]] to the [[:en:Tang dynasty|Tang dynasty]] as "medieval," while scholars from the [[Rekiken School]] viewed the Tang dynasty as part of antiquity, with the [[:en:Song dynasty|Song dynasty]] marking the start of the medieval period. Nishijima was aligned with the Rekiken School.
In his early works (referred to as the "Old Nishijima Theory"), he argued that the retinue of the [[:en:Han Gaozu|Han Gaozu]] (such as eunuchs, retainers, and soldiers) represented household slaves and pseudo-familial members. He suggested that the [[:en:Han dynasty|Han dynasty]] was a slave state, with a pseudo-family structure as the foundation of its socio-economic system.<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Nishijima Sadao|year=1949|title=中国古代帝国の一考察 : 漢の高祖とその功臣|journal=歴史学研究|volume=141}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Nishijima Sadao|year=1949|title=漢代の土地所有制 : 主に名田と占田について|journal=史学雑誌|volume=58|issue=1}}</ref>
Critiques from scholars such as [[Masubuchi Tatsuo]] and [[Hamaguchi Shigenori]] prompted Nishijima to revise his views. His "New Nishijima Theory" focused on the relationship between the emperor and smallholding farmers, proposing the theory of "individualized personal control." He argued that the [[twenty-Rank nobility system]] aimed to replace declining village-based social structures with state-administered systems, allowing direct control of individuals by the imperial government.<ref>{{Cite book|和書|title=中国古代帝国の形成と構造 : 二十等爵制の研究|year=1961|publisher=[[Tokyo University Press]]|author=Nishijima Sadao}}</ref>
=== Criticism and Legacy === While Nishijima’s new theory was recognized for its innovative perspective, critics like Masubuchi argued that it still relied too heavily on structural determinism and failed to adequately account for the roles of local elites and community dynamics.<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Masubuchi Tatsuo|year=1962|title=所謂東洋的専制主義と共同体|journal=一橋論叢|volume=26|issue=5|url=https://hdl.handle.net/10086/3385|doi=10.15057/3385}}</ref>
== Major Works ==
=== Books === * ''Formation and Structure of the Ancient Chinese Empire: Research on the Twenty-Rank Nobility System,'' Tokyo University Press, 1961. * ''Research on Chinese Economic History,'' Tokyo University Press, 1966. * ''History of China Vol. 2: Qin-Han Empire,'' Kodansha, 1974 / [[Kodansha Academic Library]], 1997. * ''Society and Economy in Ancient China,'' Tokyo University Press, 1981. * ''The Ancient Chinese State and the East Asian World,'' Tokyo University Press, 1983. * ''Japan’s Historical International Environment,'' Tokyo University Press, 1985. * ''Yamatai Kingdom and Wa Kingdom: Ancient Japan and East Asia,'' [[Yoshikawa Kobunkan]], 1994. * ''Studying Chinese History: My Journey with Ancient History,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1995.
=== Collected Works === * ''Selected Essays of Nishijima Sadao on East Asian History,'' 5 volumes, [[:en:Iwanami Shoten|Iwanami Shoten]], 2002.
== Commemorative Volumes == * ''The State and Farmers in East Asian History: Commemorative Essays for Nishijima Sadao’s Sixtieth Birthday,'' [[Yamakawa Shu11ansha]], 1984. * ''The Development of East Asian History and Japan: Memorial Essays for Nishijima Sadao,'' Yamakawa Shu11ansha, 2000.
== Related Links == * [https://cir.nii.ac.jp/ja/crid/1140563741720967168 Nishijima Sadao] - CiNii Research
== References == {{Reflist}}

He suggested that the [[:en:Han dynasty|Han dynasty]] was a slave state, with a pseudo-family structure as the foundation of its socio-economic system.<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Nishijima Sadao|year=1949|title=中国古代帝国の一考察 : 漢の高祖とその功臣|journal=歴史学研究|volume=141}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Nishijima Sadao|year=1949|title=漢代の土地所有制 : 主に名田と占田について|journal=史学雑誌|volume=58|issue=1}}</ref>
Critiques from scholars such as [[Masubuchi Tatsuo]] and [[Hamaguchi Shigenori]] prompted Nishijima to revise his views. His "New Nishijima Theory" focused on the relationship between the emperor and smallholding farmers, proposing the theory of "individualized personal control." He argued that the [[twenty-Rank nobility system]] aimed to replace declining village-based social structures with state-administered systems, allowing direct control of individuals by the imperial government.<ref>{{Cite book|和書|title=中国古代帝国の形成と構造 : 二十等爵制の研究|year=1961|publisher=[[Tokyo University Press]]|author=Nishijima Sadao}}</ref>
=== Criticism and Legacy === While Nishijima’s new theory was recognized for its innovative perspective, critics like Masubuchi argued that it still relied too heavily on structural determinism and failed to adequately account for the roles of local elites and community dynamics.<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Masubuchi Tatsuo|year=1962|title=所謂東洋的専制主義と共同体|journal=一橋論叢|volume=26|issue=5|url=https://hdl.handle.net/10086/3385|doi=10.15057/3385}}</ref>
== Major Works ==
=== Books === * ''Formation and Structure of the Ancient Chinese Empire: Research on the Twenty-Rank Nobility System,'' Tokyo University Press, 1961. * ''Research on Chinese Economic History,'' Tokyo University Press, 1966. * ''History of China Vol. 2: Qin-Han Empire,'' Kodansha, 1974 / [[Kodansha Academic Library]], 1997. * ''Society and Economy in Ancient China,'' Tokyo University Press, 1981. * ''The Ancient Chinese State and the East Asian World,'' Tokyo University Press, 1983. * ''Japan’s Historical International Environment,'' Tokyo University Press, 1985. * ''Yamatai Kingdom and Wa Kingdom: Ancient Japan and East Asia,'' [[Yoshikawa Kobunkan]], 1994. * ''Studying Chinese History: My Journey with Ancient History,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1995.
=== Collected Works === * ''Selected Essays of Nishijima Sadao on East Asian History,'' 5 volumes, [[:en:Iwanami Shoten|Iwanami Shoten]], 2002.
== Commemorative Volumes == * ''The State and Farmers in East Asian History: Commemorative Essays for Nishijima Sadao’s Sixtieth Birthday,'' [[Yamakawa Shu11ansha]], 1984. * ''The Development of East Asian History and Japan: Memorial Essays for Nishijima Sadao,'' Yamakawa Shu11ansha, 2000.

{{Infobox person|birth_date={{birth date|1919|6|25}}|death_date={{death date|1998|7|25}}|birth_place={{ill|Niimi|qid=Q873596}}|gender={{ill|male|qid=Q6581097}}|nationality={{ill|Empire of Japan|qid=Q188712}}; {{ill|Japan|qid=Q17}}|occupation={{ill|historian|qid=Q201788}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11628024]] Wikidata name: Sadao Nishijima [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:32, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Nishijima Sadao''' (西嶋 定生, [[:en:June 25|June 25]], [[:en:1919|1919]] – [[:en:July 25|July 25]], [[:en:1998|1998]]) was a Japanese historian specializing in ancient Chinese history. He was a professor emeritus at the [[:en:University of Tokyo|University of Tokyo]] and a leading figure in postwar Chinese historical studies, alongside scholars such as [[:en:Miyazaki Ichisada|Miyazaki Ichisada]] of [[:en:Kyoto University|Kyoto University]], [[Hori Toshikazu]] of the University of Tokyo, and [[Masubuchi Tatsuo]] of [[:en:Hitotsubashi University|Hitotsubashi University]].
== Biography and Background == Nishijima was born in [[Hongo Village, Achi District]], [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]] (now part of [[Niimi City]]). He graduated from the [[Sixth High School (former system)|Sixth High School]] and subsequently from [[:en:Tokyo Imperial University|Tokyo Imperial University]]. He was a close friend of [[Yamada Nobuo (historian)|Yamada Nobuo]], a Central Asian historian who was a classmate during their Sixth High School and university days.<ref>Yamada Nobuo Memorial Project, ''Hito to Hito,'' Eulogy by the Friend Representative, 11. 4–5.</ref>
Nishijima worked as a researcher at the [[Institute for Eastern Culture]] and the [[Institute of Oriental Culture at the University of Tokyo]]. In 1949, he was a11ointed assistant professor at the University of Tokyo’s Faculty of Letters, Department of Oriental History, and became a full professor in 1967.<ref name="kotobank">{{Kotobank|西嶋定生|2=}}</ref> He received his Doctorate in Literature in 1961 for his research on the [[Twenty Ranks of Nobility|twenty-Rank nobility system]]. After retiring in 1980, he became a professor emeritus and later taught at [[:en:Niigata University|Niigata University]] and [[Shujitsu University (formerly Shujitsu Women's University)]].
Nishijima passed away on July 25, 1998, at the age of 79.<ref name="kotobank"/>
== Research == Initially specializing in the socio-economic history of the [[:en:Ming|Ming]] and [[:en:Qing|Qing]] dynasties, Nishijima later shifted his focus to ancient history. He developed theories on the [[:en:tributary system|tributary system]] of the Chinese empire and the concept of the [[East Asian world]]. He also su11orted the theory that [[:en:Yamatai Kingdom|Yamatai Kingdom]] was located in northern Kyushu.
=== Theories on the Qin-Han Empires === Nishijima was a key participant in the [[Chinese historical periodization debate]] in Japan. The debate revolved around the fourfold division of history (ancient, medieval, early modern, and modern) based on the [[theory of stages of economic development]]. Kyoto scholars considered the period up to the Han dynasty as "ancient" and the period from the [[:en:Northern and Southern dynasties|Northern and Southern dynasties]] to the [[:en:Tang dynasty|Tang dynasty]] as "medieval," while scholars from the [[Rekiken School]] viewed the Tang dynasty as part of antiquity, with the [[:en:Song dynasty|Song dynasty]] marking the start of the medieval period. Nishijima was aligned with the Rekiken School.
In his early works (referred to as the "Old Nishijima Theory"), he argued that the retinue of the [[:en:Han Gaozu|Han Gaozu]] (such as eunuchs, retainers, and soldiers) represented household slaves and pseudo-familial members. He suggested that the [[:en:Han dynasty|Han dynasty]] was a slave state, with a pseudo-family structure as the foundation of its socio-economic system.<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Nishijima Sadao|year=1949|title=中国古代帝国の一考察 : 漢の高祖とその功臣|journal=歴史学研究|volume=141}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Nishijima Sadao|year=1949|title=漢代の土地所有制 : 主に名田と占田について|journal=史学雑誌|volume=58|issue=1}}</ref>
Critiques from scholars such as [[Masubuchi Tatsuo]] and [[Hamaguchi Shigenori]] prompted Nishijima to revise his views. His "New Nishijima Theory" focused on the relationship between the emperor and smallholding farmers, proposing the theory of "individualized personal control." He argued that the [[twenty-Rank nobility system]] aimed to replace declining village-based social structures with state-administered systems, allowing direct control of individuals by the imperial government.<ref>{{Cite book|和書|title=中国古代帝国の形成と構造 : 二十等爵制の研究|year=1961|publisher=[[Tokyo University Press]]|author=Nishijima Sadao}}</ref>
=== Criticism and Legacy === While Nishijima’s new theory was recognized for its innovative perspective, critics like Masubuchi argued that it still relied too heavily on structural determinism and failed to adequately account for the roles of local elites and community dynamics.<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Masubuchi Tatsuo|year=1962|title=所謂東洋的専制主義と共同体|journal=一橋論叢|volume=26|issue=5|url=https://hdl.handle.net/10086/3385|doi=10.15057/3385}}</ref>
== Major Works ==
=== Books === * ''Formation and Structure of the Ancient Chinese Empire: Research on the Twenty-Rank Nobility System,'' Tokyo University Press, 1961. * ''Research on Chinese Economic History,'' Tokyo University Press, 1966. * ''History of China Vol. 2: Qin-Han Empire,'' Kodansha, 1974 / [[Kodansha Academic Library]], 1997. * ''Society and Economy in Ancient China,'' Tokyo University Press, 1981. * ''The Ancient Chinese State and the East Asian World,'' Tokyo University Press, 1983. * ''Japan’s Historical International Environment,'' Tokyo University Press, 1985. * ''Yamatai Kingdom and Wa Kingdom: Ancient Japan and East Asia,'' [[Yoshikawa Kobunkan]], 1994. * ''Studying Chinese History: My Journey with Ancient History,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1995.
=== Collected Works === * ''Selected Essays of Nishijima Sadao on East Asian History,'' 5 volumes, [[:en:Iwanami Shoten|Iwanami Shoten]], 2002.
== Commemorative Volumes == * ''The State and Farmers in East Asian History: Commemorative Essays for Nishijima Sadao’s Sixtieth Birthday,'' [[Yamakawa Shu11ansha]], 1984. * ''The Development of East Asian History and Japan: Memorial Essays for Nishijima Sadao,'' Yamakawa Shu11ansha, 2000.
== Related Links == * [https://cir.nii.ac.jp/ja/crid/1140563741720967168 Nishijima Sadao] - CiNii Research
